proposals.title: The Role of Off-Centre and Satellite Black Holes in the AGN Feedback of Galaxy Groups and Clusters
proposals.motivation: In cosmological simulations, AGN feedback is a critical but poorly constrained mechanism for regulating star formation and setting the thermodynamic properties of the intracluster medium (ICM). The standard subgrid model, as implemented in codes like AREPO and used in projects like MillenniumTNG, assumes feedback energy is injected solely by the central supermassive black hole (SMBH) of the halo. However, observations suggest significant non-central AGN activity in group and cluster cores, which may contribute to heating and turbulence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that feedback from off-centre and satellite SMBHs, particularly during galaxy mergers and orbital decay, provides a spatially and temporally distinct energy input that is crucial for preventing catastrophic cooling and reproducing the observed diversity of core properties.
proposals.method: We will develop a new subgrid model for the AREPO code, extending the existing dynamical friction and AGN feedback frameworks. The model will track and allow for energy injection from all resolved SMBHs above a mass threshold within the cluster core, with feedback coupling efficiency modulated by local gas density and relative velocity. We will implement this in a suite of high-resolution 'Arkenstone'-type cosmological simulations of galaxy groups and clusters, comparing the resulting ICM profiles, star formation histories, and SMBH growth to both traditional central-only feedback runs and observational constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, simulation-based quantification of the importance of non-central AGN feedback in massive halos. It will deliver a calibrated model for community use and generate new, testable predictions for the correlation between AGN positioning, jet morphologies, and the thermodynamic state of the ICM.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has focused on the dynamics of wandering black holes, their treatment as persistent, coupled feedback sources in full cosmological hydrodynamical simulations is novel. This relaxes a core, untested assumption of virtually all contemporary galaxy formation models.

proposals.title: The Dual Role of Radio-Mode AGN Feedback: Quantifying the Conditions for Turbulent Suppression vs. Triggering of Star Formation in Cosmological Simulations
proposals.motivation: In modern galaxy formation models, feedback from active galactic nuclei (AGN) in the radio mode is essential for suppressing cooling flows in massive halos, reconciling simulated galaxies with observations. However, a long-standing theoretical question, informed by observations of jet-induced star formation in rare systems, is whether such feedback can ever compress gas and locally *enhance* star formation, creating a positive feedback loop. Resolving this ambiguity is critical for a predictive theory of galaxy evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while radio feedback predominantly suppresses star formation globally via turbulent heating and gas displacement, it can trigger localized, efficient star formation in high-density, magnetized circumgalactic gas clouds when the jet's momentum coupling and the cloud's gravitational boundedness exceed a critical threshold.
proposals.method: We will employ the high-resolution cosmological hydrodynamical simulation framework of the MillenniumTNG/Arkenstone project, extending its AGN feedback model. The model for dynamical friction on supermassive black holes will ensure accurate black hole dynamics. We will implement a subgrid model for jet-induced turbulence and compression, coupling it to a multi-phase interstellar medium model. High-resolution 'zoom-in' simulations of massive galaxy formation will be run with the AREPO code, using its moving mesh to accurately capture shocks and shear, with tracer particles to track the history of gas affected by jet events.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative mapping of the parameter space (e.g., jet power, ambient density, magnetization) that separates net positive from negative stellar feedback in radio-mode AGN. It will deliver a physically motivated subgrid prescription for AGN-triggered star formation usable in future large-volume simulations like MillenniumTNG.
proposals.novelty: While most simulation work focuses on AGN feedback solely as a quenching mechanism, this study systematically investigates its potential dual role by integrating a state-of-the-art jet model with a high-resolution, cosmological environment where both suppression and triggering mechanisms can emerge naturally.

proposals.title: Coupling Subgrid Jet Models to Semi-Analytic Galaxy Formation: A New Framework for AGN Feedback in the MillenniumTNG Universe
proposals.motivation: While modern cosmological simulations like MillenniumTNG (MTNG) resolve large-scale structure and galaxy formation, the detailed physics of AGN jets—critical for quenching massive galaxies—occurs below the resolution limit. Semi-analytic models (SAMs) applied to MTNG use simplified prescriptions for AGN feedback, lacking a physical description of jet propagation, lobe inflation, and their coupling to the circumgalactic medium (CGM). Incorporating well-tested subgrid jet models from high-resolution hydrodynamics is essential to predict realistic galaxy properties and the thermodynamic state of groups and clusters.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding a calibrated, Lagrangian subgrid model for relativistic jet propagation and lobe evolution into the SAM framework of MTNG, we can reproduce the observed scaling relations between black hole mass, galaxy stellar mass, and hot gas properties more accurately than with current energy-deposition schemes.
proposals.method: We will adapt the successful jet model from the AREPO-based 'Arkenstone' simulations, which tracks jet injection, lobe expansion, and bubble buoyancy in a Lagrangian fashion. This model will be integrated into the L-GALAXIES SAM applied to the MTNG dark matter merger trees. The subgrid module will use local halo properties (gas density, pressure, black hole accretion rate) as input, compute lobe dynamics and energy deposition into the CGM, and return updated gas properties to the SAM's baryonic routines. Calibration will leverage pre-2026 high-resolution jet simulations and MTNG hydrodynamical runs for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first SAM capable of predicting the detailed morphology and thermal impact of AGN jets on galaxy groups, directly linking black hole growth to observable radio lobes and X-ray cavity properties. It will yield a publicly available module for the MTNG SAM, enabling efficient exploration of jet feedback parameter space across cosmic time and improving predictions for the galaxy population in large-volume simulations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic translation of a high-fidelity, numerical jet model from a moving-mesh hydro code into a semi-analytic framework, creating a hybrid approach that combines the physical accuracy of resolved jet simulations with the statistical power and speed of SAMs applied to billion-particle N-body simulations.

proposals.title: A Unified AGN-Stellar Spectral Synthesis Model for Cosmological Galaxy Simulations
proposals.motivation: Current stellar population synthesis (SPS) models, like those used in the IllustrisTNG and MillenniumTNG projects, treat the integrated light of galaxies as a sum of purely stellar spectra. This neglects the significant contribution from active galactic nuclei (AGN), which can dominate the UV and optical emission in certain phases and bias derived galaxy properties like star formation rates and stellar masses from mock surveys. A self-consistent model for AGN-heated gas emission is critical for accurate forward modeling in next-generation cosmological simulations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating a physically motivated, time-dependent AGN spectral component into SPS libraries will significantly improve the agreement between simulated galaxy colours and observations, particularly for massive galaxies where AGN feedback is a key process in the IllustrisTNG model.
proposals.method: We will extend the existing SPS framework within the AREPO code's post-processing pipeline. The AGN spectral component will be modeled using a combination of a multi-temperature blackbody for the accretion disk and empirical templates for the broad and narrow emission line regions, scaled by the instantaneous black hole accretion rate tracked in the simulation. This component will be added to the stellar light from star particles, with dust attenuation applied consistently using the simulation's gas and metal distribution. We will validate the model by generating synthetic photometry for the MillenniumTNG simulation and comparing to observed colour-magnitude relations and AGN luminosity functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first SPS model that self-consistently unifies stellar and AGN emission for large-volume cosmological hydrodynamical simulations. It will directly enhance the realism of mock sky surveys from projects like MillenniumTNG, enabling more robust comparisons to deep extragalactic datasets from JWST, Euclid, and Rubin Observatory. The public release of the modified synthesis code will be a key tool for the community.
proposals.novelty: While AGN templates exist, their direct integration into the on-the-fly SPS machinery of a state-of-the-art cosmological simulation code, with emission tied directly to the simulated black hole accretion physics and coupled to the simulation's dust model, is a novel and necessary step for precision extragalactic forward modeling.

proposals.title: A Mass- and Redshift-Dependent Model for AGN Radio-Mode Feedback in Cosmological Simulations
proposals.motivation: In cosmological simulations, the energetic coupling of AGN radio-mode feedback is typically parameterized as a fixed fraction of the accreted rest-mass energy. However, observations of galaxy clusters suggest that the efficiency of radio bubbles in heating the intracluster medium may vary with halo mass and cosmic time. This fixed-parameter assumption is a major uncertainty in reproducing the observed galaxy and cluster scaling relations.
proposals.hypothesis: The efficiency of AGN radio-mode feedback is not a universal constant but scales with halo mass and redshift, likely decreasing in lower-mass halos and at higher redshifts due to changes in the accretion state and the ambient medium.
proposals.method: We will implement a flexible, parameterized model for the radio feedback coupling efficiency, ε_radio(M_halo, z), within the updated AREPO code and the Arkenstone galaxy formation model. Using the MillenniumTNG simulation framework, we will run a suite of cosmological hydrodynamical simulations (∼500 Mpc/h box) to test scaling relations against observational data (e.g., cluster gas fractions, entropy profiles, BCG masses). The model will be calibrated using a novel iterative approach, combining the high-resolution, full-sky weak lensing maps from MillenniumTNG for observational constraints and the well-tested dynamical friction models for SMBH dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a physically motivated, scalable prescription for radio-mode feedback that can be adopted by the community. It will improve the predictive power of simulations for the thermodynamic properties of galaxy groups and clusters across cosmic time and help resolve long-standing tensions in the stellar mass content of massive halos.
proposals.novelty: While feedback models are often tuned, this systematically tests and derives a *functional form* for the key energetic parameter based on halo properties, moving beyond a single tuned constant, which is a non-trivial extension of current practice.

proposals.title: The Impact of Non-Equilibrium Dynamics on the Hot Atmospheres of Galaxy Clusters: A Cosmological Hydrodynamical Study
proposals.motivation: The hot, X-ray-emitting atmospheres of galaxy clusters are crucial probes of cosmology and galaxy formation, but their analysis often assumes a quasi-static, relaxed state. This assumption breaks down during the frequent mergers and bulk flows that shape clusters, potentially biasing mass estimates from X-ray and Sunyaev-Zel'dovich observations. A self-consistent, cosmological framework to quantify these non-equilibrium effects is currently lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deviations from hydrostatic equilibrium, driven by mergers and large-scale motions, induce systematic, time-dependent biases in the inferred thermodynamic profiles and total masses of galaxy clusters, which can be characterized and calibrated using high-resolution simulations.
proposals.method: We will employ the state-of-the-art AREPO moving-mesh code, extending the successful Arkhestone feedback model, to run a suite of high-resolution cosmological magneto-hydrodynamical simulations (e.g., MillenniumTNG-like volumes with targeted cluster re-simulation). We will develop and implement a novel Lagrangian tracer particle scheme to track the origin and evolution of bulk and turbulent motions within the intracluster medium. This will be coupled with on-the-fly, GPU-accelerated X-ray synthetic observation pipelines to directly compare simulated dynamics with observable quantities.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, statistically robust calibration for non-thermal pressure support and X-ray property biases as a function of cluster dynamical state and redshift. It will deliver public software modules for generating realistic, non-static mock X-ray observations from cosmological simulations, directly impacting the interpretation of data from eROSITA, Athena, and CMB-S4.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have examined mergers in isolation, this proposal integrates a full cosmological history with a novel Lagrangian analysis to dissect the origins and impacts of non-equilibrium dynamics, moving beyond post-hoc analyses of static snapshots to a continuous, evolutionary picture.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Dominant Quenching Pathway: A Controlled Comparison of Radio AGN, Supernova, and Starburst Feedback in Cosmological Simulations
proposals.motivation: The quenching of star formation in massive galaxies is a cornerstone of galaxy evolution, yet the relative importance of different feedback mechanisms remains poorly quantified. While cosmological simulations like IllustrisTNG and EAGLE incorporate both stellar (supernova/winds) and AGN feedback, their complex interplay makes it difficult to isolate their individual and combined efficacy in regulating galaxy growth across cosmic time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that radio-mode AGN feedback is the dominant quenching mechanism for galaxies in halos above ~10^12 solar masses, but its efficiency is critically modulated by the pre-existing galactic environment shaped by stellar feedback, which determines the thermodynamic state of the circumgalactic medium.
proposals.method: We will employ the AREPO code and its galaxy formation model, building on the Arkenstone framework, to run a suite of high-resolution cosmological zoom-in simulations. We will systematically disable or isolate specific feedback channels (radio-mode AGN jets, thermal AGN feedback, supernova-driven winds, and starburst-driven high-specific energy winds) in otherwise identical initial conditions. The analysis will use Lagrangian tracer particles to track the energy, momentum, and metal content deposited by each mechanism, quantifying their impact on gas cooling rates, star formation histories, and the thermodynamic profiles of halos.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first controlled, quantitative decomposition of the quenching budget in simulated galaxies, directly linking specific feedback events to observable properties like hot gas X-ray luminosities and stellar population gradients. It will yield calibrated subgrid prescriptions for future large-volume simulations like MillenniumTNG, informing how to best model the transition from stellar to AGN-dominated feedback regimes.
proposals.novelty: While feedback models are often tuned to match global statistics, a controlled, pathway-specific decomposition within a state-of-the-art cosmological hydrodynamical framework has not been systematically performed, moving beyond correlation to establish causation in simulated galaxy quenching.

proposals.title: The Failure Modes of AGN Feedback: A Multi-Scale Simulation Study of Observed Cooling Flow Clusters
proposals.motivation: Despite the established role of AGN feedback in suppressing cooling flows in massive galaxy clusters, a significant population of observed clusters, such as Perseus and Abell 1795, exhibit strong cooling signatures. This indicates that the standard feedback paradigm, as implemented in cosmological simulations, is insufficient in certain regimes. Understanding why feedback fails in these systems is critical for a complete theory of galaxy formation and for interpreting observations of the intracluster medium.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the failure of AGN feedback in observed cooling flow clusters stems from a mis-match between the spatial/temporal scales of energy injection and the thermodynamic state of the core, which can be traced to specific black hole accretion dynamics and gas physics not captured in current sub-grid models.
proposals.method: We will employ the AREPO moving-mesh code and the 'Arkenstone' galaxy formation model, extending it with a calibrated dynamical friction model for supermassive black holes. We will select initial conditions matching the observed mass, redshift, and large-scale environment of known cooling flow clusters. A suite of high-resolution, 'zoomed-in' simulations will be performed, systematically varying the AGN feedback implementation (jet power, opening angle, duty cycle) and the specific energy of galactic winds. The analysis will utilize the full-physics hydrodynamical tracers and synthetic X-ray observations to directly compare with Chandra and XMM-Newton data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first self-consistent, high-fidelity simulations of specific observed cooling flow clusters, pinpointing the precise physical conditions that lead to feedback failure. It will deliver a taxonomy of failure modes and propose necessary modifications to sub-grid feedback prescriptions in next-generation cosmological simulations like MillenniumTNG.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted, observationally-anchored approach—simulating specific real clusters rather than statistical samples—and in the integrated analysis of black hole dynamics, multi-phase gas, and high-specific energy winds within a single, state-of-the-art framework to diagnose feedback inefficacy.

proposals.title: The Role of Cosmic Ray Pressure in Galaxy Formation: A New Subgrid Model for the AREPO Code
proposals.motivation: Cosmological simulations of galaxy formation have long relied on thermal and magnetic pressure to model the intracluster and circumgalactic media. However, observations, particularly from Fermi-LAT and radio synchrotron emission, indicate that cosmic rays (CRs) contribute a significant, non-thermal pressure component. This missing physics may explain long-standing discrepancies in galaxy properties, such as the overproduction of stars and the incorrect thermal structure of gaseous halos.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the injection, transport, and pressure support of cosmic rays, particularly from supernovae and AGN feedback, will significantly alter the thermodynamic state of the interstellar and circumgalactic medium, leading to more realistic galaxy masses, morphologies, and wind properties.
proposals.method: We will develop a new cosmic ray physics module for the AREPO code, building on its magnetohydrodynamics framework. The module will implement a two-fluid model (gas + CRs) with anisotropic diffusion along magnetic field lines and streaming instability-driven Alfvén wave heating, as informed by pre-2026 theoretical work. CR injection will be tied directly to the subgrid models for core-collapse supernovae and black hole accretion/feedback (e.g., the 'Arkenstone' model). We will validate the model using idealized simulations before performing full cosmological runs with the MillenniumTNG framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first self-consistent, cosmological-scale implementation of cosmic ray physics within the AREPO code, directly quantifying CR pressure's impact on galaxy formation. It will deliver publicly available subgrid models and yield predictions for non-thermal observables, such as gamma-ray emission and radio halos, that can be tested against existing multi-wavelength surveys.
proposals.novelty: While some codes incorporated basic CR advection and diffusion pre-2026, the novel integration of CR physics with AREPO's advanced black hole feedback and weak lensing ray-tracing capabilities, within a next-generation cosmological volume like MillenniumTNG, is unprecedented.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Radiative Transfer Scheme for Ionizing Radiation in Cosmological Galaxy Formation Simulations
proposals.motivation: Accurately modeling the propagation of ionizing radiation from stars and AGN is critical for understanding cosmic reionization, the thermodynamics of the circumgalactic medium, and the regulation of star formation. Current large-scale cosmological simulations, like those from the MillenniumTNG and AREPO projects, often rely on approximate, moment-based radiative transfer (RT) or sub-grid models, which lack the directional accuracy needed for the highly anisotropic radiation fields from clustered sources. This limits our ability to predict observables like Lyman-alpha forest statistics and the morphology of ionization fronts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a hybrid radiative transfer scheme, combining the efficiency of a moment-based method for the diffuse background with a ray-tracing technique for direct radiation from discrete, luminous sources, will significantly improve the fidelity of ionization and temperature fields in large-volume simulations without prohibitive computational cost.
proposals.method: We will develop and integrate this hybrid scheme into the AREPO code. For direct radiation from stellar populations and AGN (modeled as sink particles), we will employ an adaptive ray-tracing algorithm, leveraging the unstructured mesh for efficient ray traversal. The diffuse, scattered radiation field will be solved using the efficient M1 closure of the moment-based RT equations already present in AREPO. The two components will be coupled through the local ionization state and photon rates. The scheme will be tested and calibrated against established radiative transfer benchmarks (e.g., the Cosmological Radiative Transfer Comparison Project) before application in high-resolution, cosmological boxes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, high-fidelity tool for modeling ionizing radiation in next-generation galaxy formation simulations. It will enable more accurate predictions of the timing and topology of cosmic reionization and its impact on dwarf galaxy formation. Furthermore, it will allow for a direct, self-consistent comparison between simulation outputs and key observables from weak lensing and Lyman-alpha forest surveys within projects like MillenniumTNG.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid RT schemes exist, their application in large-volume, cosmological hydrodynamical simulations with full galaxy formation physics (including black hole feedback from 'Arkenstone') remains novel. The key innovation is the tight integration of a ray-tracing module with AREPO's moving mesh and its existing stellar and AGN feedback models, moving beyond post-processing or uniform background approximations.

proposals.title: Cosmic Evolution of the Stellar Initial Mass Function: A Self-Consistent, Environmentally Dependent Model for Next-Generation Cosmological Simulations
proposals.motivation: The stellar initial mass function (IMF) is a cornerstone of galaxy formation models, yet its universality is increasingly challenged by observational evidence pre-2026. Galaxy-scale variations, such as bottom-heavy IMFs in massive ellipticals and top-heavy IMFs in starbursts, suggest a strong dependence on local star-forming conditions. Ignoring this in cosmological simulations introduces systematic errors in predicted stellar masses, metallicities, feedback energetics, and supermassive black hole growth, limiting the predictive power of flagship projects like MillenniumTNG.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a physically motivated, variable IMF, parameterized by local gas properties (e.g., density, metallicity, turbulence) and cosmic time, will self-consistently reproduce observed galaxy scaling relations and resolve long-standing tensions in simulated stellar mass functions and chemical abundances.
proposals.method: We will implement a new sub-grid model for a variable IMF within the AREPO code and the Arkenstone galaxy formation model. The IMF slope will be linked to the local Jeans mass and turbulent pressure, informed by high-resolution star-formation studies. This model will be integrated into the stellar population and feedback routines, ensuring that metal yields, supernova rates, and radiative feedback from massive stars scale correctly. We will test the model in high-resolution cosmological zoom-in simulations and a new, moderate-volume (∼100 Mpc)^3 hydrodynamical simulation, comparing directly to observational constraints from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey and integral field units.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first self-consistent, cosmological-scale implementation of a physically variable IMF within a state-of-the-art moving-mesh code. It will deliver a calibrated model for the community and new, publicly available simulation data quantifying the impact of IMF variations on galaxy demographics, the baryon cycle, and the co-evolution of supermassive black holes and their hosts.
proposals.novelty: While variable IMFs have been explored in post-processing, a fully coupled, environment-dependent implementation within a high-fidelity cosmological hydrodynamical simulation, where IMF variations dynamically alter feedback and chemical evolution, is a novel and critical step forward.

proposals.title: The Cosmic Web as a Graph: A Novel Framework for Structure Classification and Dynamical Analysis in Cosmological Simulations
proposals.motivation: The cosmic web's filamentary structure is a key prediction of ΛCDM cosmology, but its analysis in simulations often relies on heuristic density-based or geometric methods like friends-of-friends or NEXUS. A more rigorous, mathematically defined framework is needed to robustly classify structures (filaments, sheets, voids, nodes) and quantify their connectivity and dynamical role in galaxy formation. This is crucial for interpreting weak lensing signals and understanding how baryons flow along dark matter scaffolds.
proposals.hypothesis: Graph theory provides a more robust and physically informative framework than traditional methods for identifying cosmic web components, and the topological properties of the resulting graph (e.g., centrality, community structure) correlate with key astrophysical processes like gas accretion, black hole fueling, and galaxy assembly.
proposals.method: We will apply graph algorithms to high-resolution dark matter and gas particle data from the MillenniumTNG and Arkenstone simulation suites. Particles or density-defined nodes will be connected into a graph using a distance or tidal tensor criterion. We will then use community detection algorithms (e.g., Louvain method) to isolate halos and subhalos, employ betweenness centrality to identify filament spines, and use minimum spanning trees to trace accretion pathways. The graph's evolution will be tracked across snapshots to study dynamical friction and wind propagation in a well-defined topological context.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, publicly available analysis pipeline for cosmological simulations that quantifies the cosmic web's topology and its connection to galaxy evolution. It will provide a clearer link between dark matter structure and baryonic observables, offering new metrics to interpret the impact of feedback from stars and black holes on the large-scale matter distribution.
proposals.novelty: While graph theory has seen limited use in cosmology (e.g., for void finding), its systematic application to classify all cosmic web components and to directly link topology to astrophysical processes within state-of-the-art hydrodynamical simulations is novel. This moves beyond geometric identification to a dynamical network analysis.

proposals.title: The Giga-Millennium: A (10 Gpc)^3 N-body and Hydrodynamical Simulation for Cosmology and the Statistics of the Rarest Objects
proposals.motivation: Current flagship cosmological simulations like MillenniumTNG, while detailed, are limited to volumes of ~(1 Gpc)^3, making them cosmic variance-limited for the largest observable modes and unable to robustly sample the rarest, most massive galaxy clusters and quasars. Upcoming surveys like Euclid, Roman, and LSST will map unprecedented volumes, demanding simulations that match their statistical power and probe the extremes of the halo mass function.
proposals.hypothesis: That a simulation with a volume ~1000 times larger than Millennium, even at lower mass resolution, will accurately capture the clustering of the largest-scale modes and the abundance and environments of the rarest, most massive objects (M_halo > 10^15 M_sun) predicted by ΛCDM.
proposals.method: We will employ the AREPO code with its novel 'Arkenstone' high-specific energy wind model for efficient feedback. Using a (10 Gpc/h)^3 volume with ~2 trillion particles, we will run a gravity-only N-body simulation, followed by a lower-resolution magneto-hydrodynamical run with baryons. The simulation will leverage the high-performance TreePM+SPH architecture of AREPO, optimized for the next generation of GPU/CPU hybrid supercomputers, to manage the immense particle count. Ray-tracing through the full lightcone will be performed using the methods developed for MillenniumTNG.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first synthetic sky survey covering the full observable volume, enabling direct, cosmic-variance-free comparisons to upcoming full-sky weak lensing and galaxy clustering data. It will provide definitive predictions for the abundance and internal structure of the rarest clusters and supermassive black hole hosts, testing ΛCDM in the high-mass regime.
proposals.novelty: While large-volume, low-resolution boxes exist, none combine this scale with AREPO's moving-mesh hydrodynamics and the sophisticated 'Arkenstone' feedback model, which is crucial for realistically modeling the galaxy population within these rare halos. The integrated full-sky ray-tracing for lensing observables at this volume is also unprecedented.

proposals.title: Inferring Galaxy Feedback Histories via Bayesian Inversion of the Stellar Mass-Halo Mass Relation from Cosmological Simulations
proposals.motivation: In cosmological simulations, feedback from stars and AGN is critical for reproducing realistic galaxy populations, but its implementation relies on subgrid models with poorly constrained parameters. Current methods tune these parameters forward in time to match observations, a process that is computationally expensive and non-unique. A robust, statistical inversion method is needed to directly constrain the feedback history required by the final galaxy population.
proposals.hypothesis: The statistical properties of the final galaxy population, specifically the stellar mass function and the stellar mass-halo mass (SMHM) relation, contain sufficient information to uniquely constrain the effective energy and momentum injection history of feedback processes in a cosmological volume.
proposals.method: We will employ the AREPO code and the 'Arkenstone' galaxy formation model as a forward model. Using the MillenniumTNG simulation suite as a prior for cosmological initial conditions and merger histories, we will develop a Bayesian inference framework. This framework will treat the key feedback parameters (e.g., wind energy, AGN coupling efficiency) as time-dependent functions. We will use Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampling to explore the high-dimensional parameter space, iteratively comparing the forward-model output to target statistics from SDSS and other pre-2026 surveys.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first self-consistent, statistically derived history of feedback energy deposition in a cosmological context. It will deliver a calibrated, time-dependent feedback model that can be directly implemented in next-generation simulations. The method will quantify the degeneracies between different feedback modes (stellar vs. AGN) across cosmic time.
proposals.novelty: While forward-tuning of feedback is standard, inverting the problem to statistically derive the required feedback history from the final galaxy population is a novel, data-constrained approach. The application of sophisticated Bayesian inference techniques to the full output of a high-resolution hydrodynamical simulation suite like MillenniumTNG is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Quantifying Non-Ideal Plasma Effects in Galaxy Clusters: A Hierarchical Bayesian Framework Applied to AREPO Simulations
proposals.motivation: The intracluster medium (ICM) is commonly modeled as an ideal gas in cosmological simulations, yet its high density and temperature suggest non-ideal effects from Coulomb interactions, partial degeneracy, and relativistic corrections may be significant. These deviations could systematically bias key observables like X-ray luminosity, SZ effect, and hydrostatic mass estimates, impacting our understanding of cluster astrophysics and cosmology. Current simulations lack a flexible, modular framework to assess the detectability and impact of these complex equation-of-state (EOS) effects.
proposals.hypothesis: Non-ideal EOS effects in the ICM produce statistically significant, detectable signatures in synthetic multi-wavelength observables, and a hierarchical Bayesian model selection can robustly identify which physical corrections are required by current and future data.
proposals.method: We will implement a modular, parameterized non-ideal EOS module within the AREPO code, building on its finite-volume magnetohydrodynamics. This module will include corrections for Coulomb logarithm, electron degeneracy, and relativistic electrons. We will generate a suite of high-resolution 'Arkenstone'-style cosmological simulations of massive galaxy clusters, varying EOS parameters. Synthetic X-ray, SZ, and weak lensing observables will be generated via forward modeling pipelines akin to MillenniumTNG. A hierarchical Bayesian inference framework, using nested sampling or Hamiltonian Monte Carlo, will then perform model selection and parameter estimation on these synthetic datasets to quantify detectability.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first principled, simulation-based assessment of which non-ideal plasma effects are observationally relevant for cluster cosmology. It will produce falsifiable predictions for XRISM, Athena, and CMB-S4, and provide a public EOS module for AREPO, enabling more physically accurate modeling of the hot plasma in galaxies and clusters.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a flexible, physics-based EOS parameterization within a state-of-the-art cosmological simulation code, coupled with a rigorous Bayesian model selection pipeline applied to synthetic observables, moves beyond ad-hoc EOS replacements to a systematic validation framework.

proposals.title: Non-Ideal Plasma Physics in Cosmological Simulations: The Impact on Intracluster Medium Thermodynamics and AGN Feedback
proposals.motivation: Modern cosmological hydrodynamical simulations, such as those from the MillenniumTNG and IllustrisTNG projects, rely on the ideal gas equation of state (EoS) for the intracluster medium (ICM). However, in the dense, hot cores of galaxy clusters, the plasma is partially degenerate and relativistic, and Coulomb interactions become significant, violating ideal gas assumptions. This inaccuracy may systematically bias predictions for ICM profiles, X-ray observables, and the coupling efficiency of AGN feedback.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a realistic, tabulated EoS for dense plasmas, accounting for partial degeneracy, relativistic electrons, and Coulomb corrections, will produce measurably different thermodynamic profiles in cluster cores and alter the dynamics of AGN feedback bubbles compared to ideal gas simulations.
proposals.method: We will implement a tabulated EoS for a fully ionized H/He plasma, based on the Chabrier & Potekhin (1998) formalism, into the AREPO code. This table will be integrated into the Riemann solver and energy equation, replacing γ=5/3. We will conduct high-resolution 'Arkenstone'-style zoom simulations of massive galaxy clusters, comparing runs with the ideal and non-ideal EoS. The impact on AGN feedback will be studied by tracking bubble expansion and energy coupling in both frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first self-consistent assessment of non-ideal plasma effects in full cosmological cluster formation simulations. It will quantify systematic shifts in predicted X-ray luminosity, Sunyaev-Zel'dovich effect signals, and entropy profiles. The results will establish whether this microphysical improvement is necessary for precision cosmology with next-generation surveys like CMB-S4 and Athena.
proposals.novelty: While realistic EoS tables exist in stellar astrophysics, their integration into large-scale cosmological simulations with complex feedback (like AGN and winds) is novel. This moves beyond isolated cluster simulations to study the effect within a full hierarchical structure formation context.

proposals.title: EntropyKinetics: A Sub-Grid Model for First-Principles Entropy Generation in Cosmological AREPO Simulations
proposals.motivation: Current cosmological hydrodynamics codes, like AREPO, treat entropy as strictly conserved outside shocks and contact discontinuities, with entropy jumps applied via Riemann solvers. This approach lacks a microphysical description of the irreversible dissipative processes within these finite-width regions, which is critical for accurately modeling the thermodynamics of galaxy formation, particularly in multi-phase media, AGN feedback, and the low-entropy gas in cluster cores.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the formalism of chemical reaction rate theory to astrophysical dissipation, we hypothesize that a sub-grid model for entropy generation rates, based on collision integrals and radiative transition probabilities, can provide a more physically grounded and accurate description of non-equilibrium thermodynamics in shocks and cooling flows than the standard instantaneous jump condition.
proposals.method: We will develop a new 'EntropyKinetics' module for the AREPO code. For shock cells flagged by the existing shock finder, we will replace the instantaneous entropy jump with a finite-volume treatment. Within these cells, we will solve coupled rate equations for entropy production from ion-electron collisions (using Braginskii-like viscosity), inelastic atomic processes (excitation, ionization), and continuum radiation, drawing on atomic data and cross-sections from pre-2026 databases (e.g., CHIANTI). The module will be tested and calibrated in isolated shock-tube and Sedov blast wave problems before integration into the full-physics galaxy formation model of the MillenniumTNG/Arkenstone framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, first-principles methodology for treating entropy in astrophysical simulations, moving beyond the standard adiabatic-plus-jumps paradigm. It will yield improved predictions for the thermal state of gas in high Mach-number shocks from AGN jets and in cooling flows, directly impacting our understanding of feedback and baryon cycling.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual shift from a discontinuity condition to a finite-rate, non-equilibrium process model, and in the direct technical adaptation of chemical kinetics formalism—with explicit entropy production rate equations—to the domain of cosmological hydrodynamics.

proposals.title: ARES: Advanced Radiative and Energetic Subgrid physics for GADGET-2 – A unified model for feedback, magnetic fields, and cosmic rays in galaxy formation
proposals.motivation: Modern cosmological simulations like MillenniumTNG and those using the Arkenstone model have highlighted the critical role of baryonic feedback in shaping galaxy masses and large-scale structure. However, the interplay between supernova/AGN feedback, dynamically important magnetic fields, and cosmic ray transport remains poorly captured in established codes like GADGET-2, limiting predictive power for observables like gas profiles and non-thermal emission.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-consistent, coupled treatment of anisotropic cosmic ray transport, small-scale magnetohydrodynamics, and high-specific-energy thermal feedback within GADGET-2 will resolve long-standing tensions in simulated galaxy scaling relations and produce more realistic intracluster and circumgalactic media.
proposals.method: We will extend GADGET-2's SPH implementation to include: 1) an anisotropic diffusion solver for cosmic rays (CRs) following the two-moment method, coupled to the thermal gas and magnetic fields; 2) a sub-grid model for magneto-rotational instability (MRI)-driven turbulent dynamo to seed and amplify magnetic fields; and 3) an updated feedback scheme where wind specific energy, as in recent Arkenstone work, is modulated by local CR pressure and B-field strength. The code will be validated against isolated galaxy simulations and then applied to high-resolution cosmological boxes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first publicly available, unified module for CR-MHD-thermal feedback within the widely used GADGET-2 framework. It will provide quantitative predictions for the impact of non-thermal physics on galaxy stellar masses, black hole growth, and the thermodynamic state of the diffuse gas, directly testable with upcoming multi-wavelength surveys.
proposals.novelty: While isolated models for these processes exist, their tight coupling within a cosmological tree-particle-mesh code like GADGET-2 is novel. The integration specifically links CR-driven winds to black hole dynamical friction models, creating a more complete picture of feedback from stellar to cluster scales.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical, Physics-Aware Time-Stepping Scheme for Galaxy Formation Simulations with AREPO
proposals.motivation: Modern cosmological simulations like MillenniumTNG and Arkenstone incorporate complex baryonic physics, including stellar feedback from supernovae. These events inject energy and momentum on extremely short timescales, creating severe stiffness that challenges standard adaptive time-stepping schemes. Current global or block-based time-stepping can either be inefficient or can artificially smooth out these crucial, highly dynamic processes, compromising the realism of galaxy formation models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical, physics-aware time-stepping scheme, which dynamically isolates and resolves localized regions of extreme energy injection, will significantly improve the accuracy and numerical stability of feedback modeling without incurring prohibitive computational cost.
proposals.method: We will develop and implement a new time integration scheme within the AREPO code. The method will extend the existing hierarchical time-step structure by introducing a physics-based trigger. Cells flagged for supernova feedback will be temporarily assigned to a much finer, localized time-step hierarchy, decoupled from the global scheme. This will be coupled with a Riemann solver-aware limiter to ensure stability during the rapid pressure changes. The scheme's performance will be rigorously tested against idealized supernova blast wave tests and within full cosmological boxes from the MillenniumTNG project.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust numerical framework for simulating violent feedback events with greater fidelity, directly impacting predictions for galaxy masses, star formation rates, and black hole growth. It will deliver a publicly available enhancement to the AREPO code, enabling more reliable studies of galaxy formation and the interplay between baryons and dark matter.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive time-stepping is standard, the novelty lies in the event-driven, physics-triggered creation of localized, temporary time-step hierarchies specifically for feedback, moving beyond purely geometry-based criteria to a scheme informed by the underlying astrophysical processes.

proposals.title: Vortex Methods for Cosmological Turbulence: A Lagrangian Approach to Galaxy Formation in AREPO
proposals.motivation: Turbulence in the circumgalactic and intracluster medium is a critical, unresolved component of galaxy formation, regulating gas cooling, star formation, and black hole accretion. Current cosmological hydrodynamics codes, including AREPO, rely on sub-grid models for turbulence which are often based on Eulerian, mesh-based approximations. A more physically grounded, Lagrangian treatment of turbulent vorticity transport could significantly improve the fidelity of feedback and mixing processes in simulations.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing vortex methods—which discretize the vorticity field into Lagrangian elements—within the moving-mesh framework of AREPO will provide a more accurate and less dissipative representation of cosmological turbulence, leading to measurably different predictions for galactic outflows and the thermodynamic state of the circumgalactic medium.
proposals.method: We will adapt the viscous vortex blob method for astrophysical fluids, integrating it as a supplementary solver within AREPO's Godunov-type scheme. The method will track vorticity generated at shocks and by shear flows, advecting vortex particles according to the local velocity field. Their mutual induction will be calculated using a fast multipole method, compatible with AREPO's tree structure. The coupling to the primary hydro solver will be managed via a source term in the momentum equation, ensuring energy and vorticity conservation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first implementation of vortex methods in a large-scale cosmological simulation code, providing a new tool to study the role of turbulence in galaxy evolution. It will produce concrete comparisons against traditional turbulent sub-grid models in the MillenniumTNG framework, quantifying impacts on observable properties like the Lyman-alpha forest and galaxy stellar mass functions.
proposals.novelty: The novel synthesis of a high-fidelity engineering fluid dynamics technique (vortex methods) with a state-of-the-art cosmological moving-mesh code (AREPO) represents a radical departure from existing, predominantly Eulerian, turbulence models in astrophysics.

proposals.title: The Spheroid as a Regulator: Testing a Causally Inverted Model for the M_BH-σ Relation in Cosmological Simulations
proposals.motivation: The tight scaling relations between supermassive black hole (SMBH) mass and host spheroid properties (e.g., velocity dispersion σ) are cornerstones of modern galaxy evolution theory. The canonical interpretation is that AGN feedback from the SMBH regulates star formation, establishing the correlation. However, the causal direction remains an assumption; an equally plausible scenario is that the gravitational potential and gas dynamics of the forming spheroid itself regulate and set the final SMBH mass.
proposals.hypothesis: The growth of central SMBHs is primarily set and limited by the evolving gravitational potential and non-axisymmetric structures (bars, mergers) of the host spheroid, which control the angular momentum loss and inflow rate of gas to the galactic center, rather than being self-regulated by AGN feedback.
proposals.method: We will employ a suite of high-resolution, cosmological hydrodynamical simulations using the AREPO code and the updated 'Arkenstone' physics model. Crucially, we will implement a novel 'inverted causality' module where SMBH accretion follows a sub-grid model directly governed by local spheroid properties (e.g., σ, binding energy, Toomre Q parameter, and torques from resolved structures), while AGN feedback is implemented in a purely 'passive', energetically consistent but non-regulatory form. This will be contrasted with control simulations using the standard feedback-regulated model. The analysis will leverage the dynamical friction model for SMBHs and the full cosmological context of the MillenniumTNG project.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first controlled, ab-initio test of the causal direction of the M_BH-σ relation. It will quantify the relative importance of spheroid-driven vs. feedback-driven regulation, offering a fundamental insight into which galactic processes are truly responsible for one of astrophysics' most famous correlations. The results could shift the paradigm for interpreting SMBH-galaxy co-evolution.
proposals.novelty: While feedback models are standard, directly inverting the causal logic in a cosmological simulation—making the spheroid the active regulator and the SMBH a passive outcome—is a radical and untested computational experiment. It moves beyond correlative studies to a causal test.

proposals.title: Predicting the Multi-Wavelength Sky: A Merger-Driven Model for Infrared and Radio Luminosity Functions from Cosmological Simulations
proposals.motivation: Current cosmological simulations, like those from the MillenniumTNG and Arkenstone projects, excel at predicting galaxy formation and dark matter structure but have a limited predictive scope for observables beyond the optical/UV. Upcoming facilities like the Vera C. Rubin Observatory, JWST, and the Square Kilometre Array (SKA) will provide unprecedented data in the infrared and radio bands, demanding robust theoretical predictions for source counts and luminosity functions to interpret the cosmic history of star formation and AGN activity.
proposals.hypothesis: The merger-driven evolutionary model for galaxies and their central supermassive black holes, calibrated in the optical, can be consistently extended to predict the statistical properties of galaxies in the infrared and radio bands by coupling simulated baryonic physics (stellar masses, star formation rates, black hole accretion) with empirical and theoretical spectral energy distribution libraries.
proposals.method: We will leverage the high-resolution, cosmological-volume hydrodynamical simulations from the MillenniumTNG and Arkenstone suites, which self-consistently model gas, stars, black holes, and feedback. For each simulated galaxy, we will assign infrared luminosities via established dust attenuation and re-emission models (e.g., based on star formation rate and metallicity) and radio luminosities using scaling relations for both star formation (radio-FIR correlation) and AGN activity (scaling with black hole accretion rate). The model will be validated against existing pre-2026 data from Herschel, ALMA, and VLA surveys before generating full-sky predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first self-consistent, large-volume predictions for infrared and radio luminosity functions directly from a state-of-the-art cosmological simulation, providing a critical theoretical framework for interpreting data from JWST and pathfinders for the SKA. It will quantify the relative contributions of merger-driven star formation and AGN activity to the cosmic background light across these wavelengths.
proposals.novelty: While simulations predict baryonic properties, a direct, statistical application of a merger-driven model to generate panchromatic (IR to radio) sky predictions from first principles, integrated with the full cosmological context of large-scale structure, remains a significant and non-trivial extension beyond current practice.

proposals.title: Deconvolving Quasar Light Curves from Luminosity Functions via Population Synthesis: A Novel Application to Cosmological Simulations
proposals.motivation: The observed quasar luminosity function (QLF) is a convolution of the underlying black hole mass function, accretion physics, and the stochastic variability of individual quasars. Disentangling these components is crucial for understanding the co-evolution of supermassive black holes and galaxies, and for interpreting the QLF as a probe of cosmological structure formation. Current models often treat quasar light curves simplistically, neglecting the full stochasticity observed in large-scale simulations.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed QLF can be accurately reproduced by modeling the underlying population of accreting black holes with stochastically varying light curves, whose statistical properties are informed by high-resolution cosmological simulations, thereby deconvolving the intrinsic variability from the population demographics.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel population synthesis framework, analogous to stellar population synthesis, but for active galactic nuclei (AGN). This will integrate: 1) A library of simulated quasar light curves from high-resolution, cosmological hydrodynamical simulations (e.g., MillenniumTNG/Arkenstone), capturing variability driven by gas dynamics and feedback. 2) A parameterized model for the underlying black hole mass function and duty cycle. 3) A forward-modeling and Bayesian inference pipeline to deconvolve the light curve properties by fitting the observed QLF across redshifts. The method will leverage the GADGET-4 and AREPO codes for simulation data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, physically grounded model linking the statistical properties of quasar variability to the ensemble QLF. It will yield constraints on the typical quasar duty cycle and light curve structure, directly informing models of black hole growth and feedback. The resulting public synthesis code will be a key tool for interpreting next-generation AGN surveys from facilities like LSST and Euclid.
proposals.novelty: Applying the core philosophy of stellar population synthesis—deconvolving an ensemble observable into its constituent physical processes—to the time-domain problem of quasar populations is a novel conceptual leap. The approach is uniquely enabled by the detailed, self-consistent quasar light curves now available from large-volume cosmological simulations.

proposals.title: A High-Resolution Shock-Capturing Scheme for the Intracluster Medium: Implementation and Impact on AGN Feedback in the AREPO Code
proposals.motivation: The intracluster medium (ICM) in galaxy clusters hosts complex, multi-phase gas dynamics, including shocks and turbulence driven by active galactic nuclei (AGN) feedback and structure formation. Accurately capturing these discontinuities is critical for modeling the thermodynamic state of the ICM, which governs observable X-ray and Sunyaev-Zel'dovich effects. Current cosmological simulations with approximate Riemann solvers or artificial viscosity may overly diffuse these features, limiting predictive power for cluster scaling relations and feedback efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a high-resolution, shock-capturing Riemann solver (e.g., HLLC or HLLD) within the moving-mesh AREPO code will significantly improve the fidelity of ICM thermodynamics, leading to more physically realistic AGN feedback bubbles, cold fronts, and merger shocks compared to existing methods.
proposals.method: We will implement and benchmark the HLLC (Harten-Lax-van Leer Contact) and HLLD (Harten-Lax-van Leer Discontinuities) Riemann solvers within the AREPO code's hydrodynamic kernel, building on its existing finite-volume Godunov framework. The implementation will include appropriate limiters (e.g., MUSCL) to achieve second-order accuracy in space and time. We will conduct controlled tests (e.g., Sod shock tube, Sedov blast wave) and cosmological simulations of a single massive galaxy cluster, comparing the new solvers directly with AREPO's standard solver and the results from the recent Arkenstone feedback model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, high-fidelity hydrodynamic module for the AREPO code, specifically optimized for the low-density, high-Mach number regime of the ICM. It will deliver new insights into how numerical dissipation affects the morphology and energy distribution of AGN-driven outflows and merger shocks, leading to more robust predictions for multi-wavelength cluster observables.
proposals.novelty: While high-resolution shock-capturing schemes are standard in idealized astrophysics, their careful integration and performance analysis within a state-of-the-art, cosmological moving-mesh code like AREPO, with a direct focus on the ICM and AGN feedback, is a non-trivial and impactful advancement.

proposals.title: Orbital Phase-Space Classification in Cosmological Clusters: A Dynamical Systems Analysis of Galaxy Trajectories in MillenniumTNG
proposals.motivation: Understanding the orbital dynamics of galaxies within clusters is crucial for interpreting cluster assembly, galaxy quenching, and the efficiency of dynamical friction. Current analyses in cosmological simulations like MillenniumTNG often rely on simplified orbital parameters (e.g., eccentricity, pericenter) without a rigorous framework for classifying orbital stability, chaos, and phase-space mixing, which govern long-term evolution and substructure survival.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant fraction of galaxy orbits in cluster environments, particularly for infalling groups and massive satellites, exhibit chaotic dynamics due to the time-varying, multi-scale cluster potential, and that this chaos accelerates phase-mixing and influences the efficacy of dynamical friction on their central black holes.
proposals.method: We will post-process high-temporal-resolution merger trees and particle data from the full-physics hydrodynamical MillenniumTNG simulations. For selected cluster halos, we will reconstruct the time-dependent gravitational potential. We will compute orbit classifications using Lyapunov exponents and frequency map analysis, implemented within the GADGET-4 analysis framework. This will be cross-correlated with subhalo properties (mass, star formation history) and the evolution of embedded supermassive black holes, building on Springel's calibrated dynamical friction model.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic map of orbital classes (regular, resonant, chaotic) for galaxies in a state-of-the-art cosmological volume. It will quantify the impact of orbital chaos on satellite stripping and black hole orbital decay, providing a dynamical foundation for interpreting cluster galaxy populations and refining semi-analytic models.
proposals.novelty: While dynamical systems theory is standard in stellar dynamics, its application to the complex, time-dependent potentials of cosmological cluster formation, using the full phase-space information of modern hydrodynamical simulations, is novel and moves beyond parameterized orbit descriptions.

proposals.title: The Impact of High-Specific-Energy Winds on Baryon Cycling and Black Hole Dynamics in Galaxy Groups
proposals.motivation: Cosmological simulations have made significant progress in modeling massive galaxy clusters and field galaxies, but the intermediate galaxy group regime (M_halo ~ 10^13-10^14 M_sun) remains a critical testing ground for feedback models. Pre-2026, the Arkenstone simulation framework demonstrated that high-specific-energy winds can effectively regulate star formation in massive halos, but their efficacy and impact on black hole growth and dynamical friction in the lower-potential wells of groups are untested.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in galaxy groups, the high-specific-energy wind feedback model, calibrated for clusters, will produce a more realistic baryon fraction and intra-group medium (IGM) while significantly altering the dynamical evolution of supermassive black holes (SMBHs) compared to standard feedback schemes.
proposals.method: We will employ the AREPO moving-mesh code with the updated 'Arkenstone' physics model, including its high-specific-energy wind feedback. A new, high-resolution cosmological volume will be simulated, targeting the formation of ~50 galaxy groups. We will implement the calibrated N-body dynamical friction model for SMBHs from recent work to track their orbital decay. The analysis will compare IGM properties, stellar mass functions, and SMBH dynamics against both cluster-scale Arkenstone runs and observational group catalogs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed predictions for the thermodynamical state of the IGM and the SMBH merger rates in a cosmologically representative sample of simulated galaxy groups using a state-of-the-art feedback model. It will establish whether a single feedback prescription can scale across halo masses or if group-specific adjustments are required.
proposals.novelty: While groups are often simulated, applying the specifically tuned 'preventative' high-energy wind model from cluster-scale simulations to this mass scale, coupled with a precise treatment of SMBH dynamical friction, is a novel and stringent test of feedback scalability.

proposals.title: Beyond a Single Timescale: A Multi-Environment Model for Star Formation in Cosmological Simulations
proposals.motivation: State-of-the-art cosmological simulations like IllustrisTNG and MillenniumTNG rely on a single, calibrated free parameter (e.g., a gas depletion timescale) to regulate star formation in resolved interstellar media. This simplification is at odds with extensive pre-2026 observational evidence showing that star formation efficiency varies systematically with galactic environment, local gas properties, and dynamical state. A unified model that captures these dependencies is crucial for robustly predicting galaxy demographics and connecting simulations to multi-wavelength surveys.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the single star formation timescale with an environmentally dependent model, informed by local gas dynamics and thermodynamic state, will significantly improve the match between simulated and observed galaxy scaling relations across diverse environments, from cluster cores to field galaxies.
proposals.method: We will implement and test new subgrid models within the AREPO code framework. The model will parameterize the star formation efficiency per free-fall time as a function of local environmental variables such as gas turbulent velocity dispersion, the ratio of rotational to gravitational energy, and ambient pressure, calibrated against resolved ISM simulations and extragalactic observations. These models will be integrated into the sophisticated baryonic physics suite of the MillenniumTNG/Arkenstone pipeline, allowing direct comparison of galaxy populations, star formation histories, and metal enrichment against the fiducial single-parameter model in large-volume cosmological runs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a more physically grounded and flexible star formation model for next-generation cosmological simulations. It will provide a clearer theoretical link between galaxy-scale observables and the small-scale physics of the ISM, and yield improved predictions for the star-forming main sequence, the quenched fraction in clusters, and the morphological mix of galaxies.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have explored environmental effects in post-processing or idealized setups, this proposal integrates a dynamic, locally calculated star formation efficiency directly into a full-physics cosmological simulation code used for flagship projects, moving beyond static, globally calibrated parameters.

proposals.title: The Role of Shear and Turbulence in Cold Cloud Destruction: A New Subgrid Model for Cosmological Galaxy Formation Simulations
proposals.motivation: In cosmological simulations, the survival and growth of cold, dense gas clouds in the circumgalactic medium (CGM) is critical for regulating star formation and black hole fueling. Pre-2026 models, like those in the IllustrisTNG and related projects, often treat cloud destruction primarily via thermal evaporation by supernova-heated gas. However, observations and high-resolution local simulations suggest that Kelvin-Helmholtz instabilities driven by shear and turbulence may be the dominant destruction mechanism, a process poorly captured in large-volume simulations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a subgrid model for cold cloud destruction driven primarily by resolved and subgrid turbulent and shear velocities will fundamentally alter the predicted cold gas content, star formation rates, and feedback cycles in galaxies, bringing simulations into better agreement with observed CGM properties.
proposals.method: We will develop and implement a new Lagrangian subgrid model within the AREPO code, building on the existing framework for galactic winds and black hole dynamics. The model will compute cloud mass loss rates based on local velocity shear and turbulent energy density, using a calibrated prescription derived from high-resolution 'cloud-crushing' simulations. This will be integrated into the baryonic physics modules of the MillenniumTNG simulation suite, allowing us to run controlled cosmological experiments comparing the new shear/turbulence-driven destruction model against the traditional thermal evaporation model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a physically motivated, calibrated model for cold cloud lifecycle in the CGM, a key uncertainty in galaxy formation theory. It will directly impact predictions for gas accretion onto galaxies, the multiphase structure of the CGM, and the efficiency of stellar and AGN feedback in regulating galaxy growth.
proposals.novelty: While cloud destruction by shear is studied in idealized setups, its systematic implementation as the primary destruction channel in full cosmological, galaxy formation simulations represents a significant paradigm shift from the dominant supernova-heating framework used in major simulation projects pre-2026.

proposals.title: Bursty vs. Quiescent: A Systematic Investigation of Star Formation Regimes in Dwarf Galaxies and Starbursts with the AREPO Code
proposals.motivation: Cosmological simulations often adopt a 'self-regulated, quiescent' mode for star formation, where the Kennicutt-Schmidt law emerges from equilibrium assumptions. However, observations of dwarf galaxies and starbursts reveal highly bursty, non-equilibrium star formation histories. This discrepancy suggests that the universality of the quiescent mode is an oversimplification, potentially biasing our understanding of galaxy assembly, feedback efficiency, and the baryon cycle in low-mass and extreme environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that star formation in dwarf galaxies and starbursts is fundamentally non-quiescent, governed by bursty, stochastic processes that cannot be captured by equilibrium-based sub-grid models, leading to systematically different galaxy properties compared to simulations enforcing universal self-regulation.
proposals.method: We will employ the AREPO moving-mesh code to run a suite of high-resolution, cosmological zoom-in simulations, targeting dwarf galaxies and merger-driven starbursts. We will implement and test a new, explicitly non-equilibrium star formation model where the efficiency is a dynamic function of local gas properties and recent feedback history, inspired by the 'Prevention is better than cure' Arkenstone feedback model. This will be coupled with a refined treatment of multi-phase ISM physics and compared against control runs using a standard, equilibrium-based star formation prescription. The analysis will focus on quantifying the temporal power spectra of star formation rates, gas depletion times, and the coupling efficiency of stellar feedback.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, simulation-based framework to classify star formation regimes (bursty vs. quiescent) across galaxy mass and type. It will deliver calibrated sub-grid recommendations for simulating non-equilibrium star formation, directly impacting predictions for dwarf galaxy demographics, the low-mass end of the stellar mass function, and the interpretation of starburst phenomena in upcoming surveys.
proposals.novelty: While burstiness is acknowledged, this project systematically challenges the foundational *universality* of the quiescent mode in simulation sub-grid physics, moving from phenomenological observation to a first-principles implementation and test within a state-of-the-art cosmological code.

proposals.title: Constraining Cosmological Parameters via the Stellar Mass Crisis: An Inverse Approach with Hybrid Hydrodynamical Simulations
proposals.motivation: Modern cosmological hydrodynamical simulations, such as IllustrisTNG and EAGLE, have demonstrated a persistent 'stellar mass crisis,' where simulated galaxies overproduce stellar mass compared to observations. This tension is a critical failure mode of the current ΛCDM paradigm coupled with state-of-the-art subgrid models for baryonic physics. Resolving whether this discrepancy points to flaws in cosmological parameters (e.g., σ₈, Ωₘ) or in our understanding of baryon physics (e.g., feedback) is a fundamental pre-2026 challenge for theoretical cosmology.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed galaxy stellar mass function provides a stringent, low-redshift constraint that can break degeneracies between cosmological parameters and the efficiency of baryonic feedback, allowing us to infer preferred regions of the (σ₈, Ωₘ) plane or identify necessary revisions to wind and AGN feedback models.
proposals.method: We will employ the AREPO code and the 'Arkenstone' simulation framework to run a suite of cosmological 'zoom-in' simulations of Milky Way-mass halos, systematically varying both cosmological parameters (σ₈, Ωₘ) and key feedback parameters governing high specific energy winds (as in Springel et al. 2025). We will use the well-calibrated model for dynamical friction from supermassive black holes to ensure accurate BH dynamics. A Bayesian inference pipeline, combining Gaussian Process emulators for simulation outputs and Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling, will be used to invert the stellar mass function constraint and derive posterior probabilities for the input parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative, simulation-based posterior constraints on cosmology from galaxy formation data, moving beyond forward modeling. It will deliver a publicly available emulator for stellar mass as a function of cosmology and feedback, and provide clear guidance on whether resolving the stellar mass crisis requires a cosmological shift or more radical feedback prescriptions.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies adjusted feedback to match cosmology, this proposal inverts the problem, treating the stellar mass function as an inverse constraint on cosmology itself. It uniquely integrates the latest models for black hole dynamical friction and high-energy winds into a rigorous statistical inference framework designed for this specific tension.

proposals.title: Beyond Bondi: A Subgrid Model for Angular Momentum-Limited Accretion onto SMBHs in Cosmological Galaxy Formation Simulations
proposals.motivation: In cosmological simulations, supermassive black hole (SMBH) accretion is commonly modeled via the Bondi-Hoyle formula, which neglects angular momentum. This is a critical oversimplification, as angular momentum forms a barrier that can suppress accretion by orders of magnitude compared to Bondi estimates, directly impacting SMBH growth and feedback in galaxy formation models. A physically motivated subgrid model for angular momentum-limited accretion is therefore essential for next-generation simulations like MillenniumTNG and its successors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a subgrid accretion model based on viscous transport through a resolved, subgrid accretion disk—where the accretion rate is set by the local gas angular momentum and viscosity—will yield SMBH growth and AGN feedback signatures fundamentally different from, and more realistic than, those predicted by standard Bondi-based models.
proposals.method: We will develop and implement a new module for the AREPO code. The model will compute the specific angular momentum of gas within the SMBH's accretion region, construct a subgrid thin α-disk, and calculate the viscous inflow rate as the accretion rate onto the BH. This will be coupled to existing stellar and AGN feedback models (e.g., Arkenstone's high-energy winds). We will test the model in isolated galaxy and cosmological zoom simulations, comparing directly to Bondi-based runs and calibrating against observed SMBH-galaxy scaling relations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a robust, publicly available subgrid model for non-Bondi SMBH accretion, directly implementable in large-scale cosmological simulations. It will yield more accurate predictions for SMBH mass assembly histories and the impact of AGN feedback on galaxy evolution, particularly in regulating star formation in massive galaxies.
proposals.novelty: While angular momentum's role is known, a practical, cosmology-ready subgrid model that self-consistently links large-scale gas properties to a viscous disk accretion rate within a moving-mesh hydrodynamics code like AREPO does not exist. This moves beyond phenomenological suppression factors to a physics-based prescription.

proposals.title: Momentum-Driven Winds from AGN: A Radiative Transfer Framework for Cosmological Galaxy Formation Simulations
proposals.motivation: In cosmological simulations, AGN feedback is a critical but crudely modeled process, often implemented as purely thermal energy injection. Observations, however, suggest that radiation pressure on gas and dust, and the resulting momentum-driven winds, are a primary mechanism for regulating black hole growth and quenching star formation. The lack of explicit radiative transfer in large-volume simulations like MillenniumTNG limits our understanding of how AGN luminosity couples to galactic-scale outflows.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing thermally coupled AGN feedback with a radiative transfer scheme that directly computes radiation pressure and multi-frequency photon-matter interactions will produce more physically accurate, momentum-driven galactic winds and significantly alter the predicted galaxy luminosity function and black hole-host galaxy scaling relations.
proposals.method: We will implement a novel hybrid radiative transfer (RT) module within the AREPO code, building on the infrastructure developed for the MillenniumTNG project. The module will use an implicit monochromatic RT solver for the UV/optical bands coupled to the non-equilibrium chemistry network to track radiation pressure on neutral gas and dust. For the ionizing X-ray band, we will employ a ray-tracing method, similar to that used for weak lensing, to deposit energy and momentum in a spatially resolved manner, linking it to the calibrated dynamical friction model for black holes. This RT-coupled feedback will replace the purely thermal 'Arkenstone' model in new high-resolution cosmological boxes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first self-consistent, radiative-transfer-based model for AGN feedback in large-volume cosmological hydrodynamical simulations. It will provide quantitative predictions for wind velocities, mass loading factors, and the impact on the circumgalactic medium, directly testable against IFU observations. The resulting public simulation data will be a key resource for interpreting multi-wavelength galaxy surveys.
proposals.novelty: While some sub-grid models include a 'momentum boost,' this proposal integrates full spectral radiative transfer directly into the gravity-hydrodynamics solver of a flagship cosmological simulation code, moving beyond prescribed coupling efficiencies to a first-principles calculation of the radiation field's dynamical impact.

proposals.title: High-Resolution Galaxy Merger Simulations with AREPO-AMR: Resolving Dynamical Friction and Black Hole Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Cosmological simulations like MillenniumTNG have successfully modeled large-scale structure, but the detailed physics of galaxy mergers—critical for black hole (BH) pairing, dynamical friction, and star formation—are often under-resolved. The fixed mass resolution of traditional Tree-PM/SPH methods limits the study of small-scale interaction regions where key processes occur. Achieving adaptive, high resolution specifically in these regions is a major unsolved challenge for understanding galaxy formation.
proposals.hypothesis: By implementing a flexible Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) scheme within the moving-mesh AREPO code, we can achieve orders-of-magnitude higher resolution in merger interfaces and galactic cores, leading to a more accurate and physically detailed model of dynamical friction, BH orbital decay, and feedback-driven winds during interactions.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid scheme integrating AMR into AREPO's unstructured Voronoi mesh. Refinement criteria will be based on local density, velocity shear, and the presence of sink particles (BHs and star clusters). The gravitational force calculation will be adapted, using a multigrid solver for the refined mesh regions while preserving the Tree-PM approach for long-range forces. We will test the implementation using controlled merger simulations from the IllustrisTNG suite, comparing BH dynamics directly against the calibrated dynamical friction model from Springel et al. (2025).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available version of AREPO with integrated AMR, enabling the community to simulate galaxy mergers with unprecedented local resolution. It will deliver new insights into the efficiency of dynamical friction for supermassive BH binaries and the impact of merger-triggered, high-specific-energy winds (as in Arkenstone) on the circumgalactic medium.
proposals.novelty: While AMR is standard in grid-based codes (e.g., ENZO), its integration into a moving, unstructured mesh geometry like AREPO's is non-trivial and represents a significant algorithmic advance.
