This hybrid approach aims to combine the Lagrangian advantages of AREPO for smooth flows with the targeted resolution of AMR at discontinuities and high-density peaks.

proposals.title: Coupling Parsec-Scale Radiatively Inefficient Accretion Flows to Galaxy-Scale Simulations: A Subgrid Model for Black Hole Feedback from First Principles
proposals.motivation: Cosmological simulations like IllustrisTNG and MillenniumTNG rely on subgrid models for AGN feedback, where wind properties are prescribed based on accretion rates. However, the physical link between the sub-parsec accretion disk physics and the resulting large-scale, high-energy winds remains poorly constrained. Resolving this connection is critical for predicting the impact of supermassive black holes on galaxy formation, particularly in regulating star formation and black hole growth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subgrid model derived from high-resolution, local simulations of radiatively inefficient accretion flows (RIAFs) can produce AGN-driven winds with properties (mass loading, specific energy, momentum flux) that differ significantly from standard prescriptions, leading to a more realistic and self-regulated coupling between black holes and their host galaxies.
proposals.method: We will perform a suite of high-resolution, 3D general relativistic magnetohydrodynamic (GRMHD) simulations of RIAFs around spinning black holes using the BHAC code, resolving the accretion disk and jet/wind launching region down to the event horizon. These simulations will be post-processed to measure wind properties as a function of accretion rate and black hole spin. The resulting scaling relations will be implemented as a new, physically motivated subgrid module within the AREPO code, replacing the existing thermal and kinetic feedback modes. This module will be tested in isolated galaxy and cosmological zoom simulations from the MillenniumTNG/Arkenstone framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first subgrid AGN feedback model for cosmological simulations explicitly calibrated to first-principles, parsec-scale GRMHD simulations. It will provide a robust theoretical prediction for the specific energy and geometry of AGN winds, directly addressing the 'prevention vs. cure' feedback dichotomy explored in recent work. The resulting public module will be a significant step towards ab initio modeling of black hole-galaxy co-evolution.
proposals.novelty: While GRMHD simulations of accretion disks exist, and cosmological simulations use AGN feedback, no model has directly coupled the wind outputs from horizon-scale GRMHD simulations into a full cosmological galaxy formation code like AREPO to self-consistently drive galactic-scale outflows.

proposals.title: Non-linear from the outset: Cosmological signatures of strong primordial perturbations in galaxy formation
proposals.motivation: The standard cosmological model assumes Gaussian, linear (weak) primordial perturbations. However, alternative models of inflation or early-universe physics can generate strong, non-Gaussian initial conditions. The impact of such 'strong ripples' on the non-linear structure formation of galaxies and black holes remains poorly explored with modern, high-fidelity simulations, creating a gap in our understanding of early-universe constraints from late-time observables.
proposals.hypothesis: Strong, non-linear primordial perturbations will leave distinct, non-degenerate imprints on the abundance, clustering, and internal structure of early-forming dark matter halos, galaxies, and their central supermassive black holes, which are not reproducible by simply rescaling standard weak perturbation models.
proposals.method: We will generate novel initial conditions with strong, non-Gaussian perturbations using the Zeldovich approximation and higher-order Lagrangian perturbation theory, implemented within the AREPO code's framework. These will drive a suite of high-resolution, cosmological hydrodynamical simulations (extending the 'Arkenstone' model) incorporating baryonic physics, stellar feedback, and black hole growth. We will employ the sophisticated ray-tracing and Born-approximation pipelines from MillenniumTNG to produce synthetic observables (weak lensing, galaxy clustering) and calibrate dynamical friction models for black hole mergers in these non-standard backgrounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first self-consistent, high-resolution simulation library mapping strong primordial perturbations to late-time galaxy and black hole populations. It will deliver concrete, observable signatures for next-generation surveys (e.g., Euclid, Roman) to confirm or rule out classes of early-universe models, moving beyond CMB-only constraints.
proposals.novelty: While past studies examined non-Gaussianity in dark-matter-only contexts, this proposal uniquely integrates state-of-the-art galaxy formation physics and observable-generation tools to test strong perturbations, where the linear approximation breaks down at the epoch of initial condition generation itself.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Role of Non-Gravitational Feedback in Cosmic Structure Amplification: A New Suite of Arepo Simulations
proposals.motivation: While gravity is the dominant driver of cosmic structure formation, the observed properties of galaxies and the intracluster medium cannot be explained by gravity alone. Pre-2026 cosmological simulations, including the MillenniumTNG and IllustrisTNG projects, have shown that feedback from stars and supermassive black holes (SMBHs) profoundly shapes galaxies, but a systematic, large-volume study isolating their role in *amplifying* large-scale structure (e.g., enhancing clustering, altering halo mass functions) is lacking. This work aims to directly test if these non-gravitational forces act as a significant secondary amplifier of structure on cosmological scales.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that feedback processes, particularly high-specific energy winds from active galactic nuclei (AGN) and supernovae, do not merely redistribute baryons but actively amplify the growth of cosmic structure by pre-heating and modifying the accretion history of dark matter halos, leading to measurable deviations in clustering statistics and halo mass functions compared to gravity-only simulations.
proposals.method: We will employ the Arepo moving-mesh code to run a new suite of large-volume cosmological simulations, building on the Arkenstone model. The core methodology involves running three simulation variants with identical initial conditions: (1) a gravity-only (dark matter + passive gas) run, (2) a run with stellar feedback only, and (3) a full-physics run including the calibrated AGN feedback model from recent SMBH dynamical friction studies. We will use the full-sky ray-tracing capabilities developed for MillenniumTNG to construct weak lensing maps from each variant, enabling a direct comparison of projected mass distributions. Statistical analysis will focus on the matter power spectrum, halo mass function, and galaxy clustering, using the gravity-only run as a baseline to isolate the amplification signal from feedback.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, large-scale measure of how non-gravitational feedback amplifies (or suppresses) cosmic structure across a wide mass and redshift range. It will deliver concrete scaling relations for this amplification effect, offering a critical test for galaxy formation models and potentially explaining tensions in $\sigma_8$ measurements between different cosmological probes. The resulting public simulation data will be a key resource for interpreting future weak lensing surveys like Euclid and LSST.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the perspective from feedback as a *regulator* of galaxy formation to a potential *amplifier* of large-scale structure itself, and in designing a controlled numerical experiment to isolate this effect using state-of-the-art, multi-physics simulations at cosmological volumes.

proposals.title: Anisotropic Inflationary Initial Conditions for Cosmological Simulations: Effects on Large-Scale Structure and Black Hole Formation
proposals.motivation: The standard inflationary paradigm posits a homogeneous and isotropic rapid expansion, seeding nearly scale-invariant Gaussian perturbations. However, pre-2026 theoretical work explores models where inflation is driven by vector fields or occurs in a Bianchi-type geometry, predicting statistically anisotropic primordial power spectra. Testing the observational signatures of such early-universe anisotropy requires embedding it in state-of-the-art cosmological simulations to derive falsifiable predictions for galaxy formation and large-scale structure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a statistically anisotropic component in the primordial density field, generated by a phase of anisotropic inflation, will imprint specific, measurable signatures on the large-scale clustering of galaxies and the early formation sites of supermassive black holes.
proposals.method: We will modify the initial condition generator of the AREPO code to implement a primordial power spectrum with a quadrupolar anisotropy of the form P(k) = P_iso(k)[1 + g_* (k̂·d̂)^2], calibrated from inflationary models. We will run a suite of high-resolution, full-physics cosmological simulations (e.g., MillenniumTNG/Arkenstone framework), varying the anisotropy amplitude (g_*) and direction (d̂). The analysis will employ ray-tracing for weak lensing maps and the calibrated dynamical friction model to track black hole dynamics in these anisotropic environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first set of large-scale structure simulations with controlled anisotropic initial conditions, providing concrete predictions for galaxy clustering, weak gravitational lensing, and black hole demographics as a function of sky direction. It will establish quantitative links between fundamental early-universe physics and late-time observables, guiding searches for statistical anisotropy in upcoming surveys like Euclid and LSST.
proposals.novelty: While anisotropic inflation has been studied theoretically, its consequences have not been rigorously explored with modern, galaxy formation-resolving hydrodynamical simulations. This proposal uniquely combines Springel's expertise in simulation infrastructure and black hole physics to confront a foundational cosmological assumption with nonlinear astrophysical observables.

proposals.title: Constraining Primordial Fluctuation Amplification with Full-Sky Weak Lensing and Galaxy Clustering from the MillenniumTNG Simulations
proposals.motivation: The growth of cosmic structure from weak primordial ripples, as predicted by inflation, is a cornerstone of the ΛCDM model. However, the detailed non-linear amplification process, particularly the interplay between dark matter, baryons, and feedback, remains a critical uncertainty in connecting early-universe physics to the low-redshift universe. Directly testing this amplification against observations requires exquisitely detailed, multi-physics simulations and synthetic observables that can be compared to next-generation surveys like Euclid and LSST.
proposals.hypothesis: The non-linear gravitational amplification of primordial density fluctuations, as modeled by state-of-the-art cosmological simulations, will produce a unique, detectable signature in the joint statistics of full-sky weak gravitational lensing and galaxy clustering that is consistent with current and forthcoming observational data.
proposals.method: We will leverage the flagship MillenniumTNG (MTNG) suite of simulations, which combines a large-volume N-body simulation (MTNG740) with high-resolution, full-physics hydrodynamical runs (MTNG-Hydro). Using the ARKENSTONE galaxy formation model, we will generate synthetic galaxy catalogs and full-sky, high-resolution weak lensing maps via on-the-fly ray-tracing. We will compute two- and three-point correlation functions (e.g., shear-shear, galaxy-shear) from these synthetic skies and compare them directly to observational data from KiDS, DES, and early Euclid data releases. A key technical focus will be quantifying the impact of baryonic feedback on the amplification signal via controlled comparisons between the hydrodynamical and dark-matter-only runs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the most comprehensive test to date of the non-linear structure growth model in ΛCDM, using the joint constraining power of lensing and clustering. It will deliver precise theoretical predictions for upcoming surveys and place new constraints on baryonic feedback processes by isolating their imprint on the amplified large-scale structure. The resulting synthetic sky catalogs and analysis pipelines will be a public resource for the community.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined structure growth in simulations, this proposal uniquely combines the unprecedented volume and resolution of MTNG with its sophisticated ARKENSTONE feedback model and full-sky ray-tracing to perform a direct, tomographic, multi-probe observational test of the entire amplification history from z~20 to z=0.

proposals.title: Constraining Dark Matter Halo Concentrations with Multi-Probe Synthetic Observations from the MillenniumTNG Simulations
proposals.motivation: The concentration of dark matter halos is a key prediction of cosmological models, encoding information about the assembly history of structure. However, observational constraints from pre-2026 data, such as weak lensing or galaxy kinematics, are often indirect and subject to significant systematic uncertainties related to baryonic physics. A unified, multi-probe framework is needed to map halo concentrations more precisely and test the Lambda-CDM paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: By combining high-resolution, full-physics hydrodynamical simulations with synthetic observables for weak lensing, galaxy clustering, and the kinematics of satellite galaxies, we can derive significantly tighter and more robust constraints on the concentration-mass relation of dark matter halos across cosmic time.
proposals.method: We will leverage the new, large-volume MillenniumTNG simulations (MTNG), which include the full-physics hydrodynamical run MTNG740 and its dark-matter-only counterpart. We will generate synthetic weak lensing maps using the on-the-fly ray-tracing capabilities developed for MTNG, moving beyond the Born approximation. For selected massive halos, we will create mock kinematic tracers of the gravitational potential by modeling observable satellite galaxies and intra-cluster light, applying the calibrated dynamical friction model for black holes to track tracers more accurately. A Bayesian inference framework will then jointly fit the multi-wavelength synthetic data to extract halo concentrations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, publicly available library of multi-probe synthetic data for halo concentration studies. It will quantify the relative constraining power of different observational techniques and establish a robust benchmark for interpreting upcoming data from Euclid and the Rubin Observatory. The results will directly test predictions for halo concentration from different feedback implementations in cosmological simulations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated, self-consistent use of the latest full-physics simulations (MTNG) to generate and jointly analyze multiple observational probes (weak lensing, satellite kinematics) specifically for concentration mapping, moving beyond single-method studies and isolated halo analyses.

proposals.title: Cosmological Gas Fragmentation as a Non-Equilibrium Phase Transition: A Condensed Matter Approach in the MillenniumTNG Framework
proposals.motivation: In cosmological simulations, the transition from diffuse gas to dense, star-forming clouds is a critical but poorly resolved process, often governed by sub-grid models. The physics of fragmentation and condensation shares conceptual parallels with nucleation and spinodal decomposition in condensed matter systems, yet these formalisms are rarely applied to the multi-scale, turbulent, and cosmologically expanding interstellar medium. A rigorous, physics-based model for this transition is essential for predicting the initial mass function of stars and the fueling of supermassive black holes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fragmentation of galactic gas into star-forming clumps and filaments can be quantitatively described as a first-order phase transition, where the interplay between cooling, turbulence, and gravitational instability sets the effective 'temperature' and 'pressure' governing nucleation rates and critical cluster sizes.
proposals.method: We will implement a novel sub-grid model within the AREPO code and the Arkenstone feedback framework, treating the dense, cold gas phase as a condensing material. The model will adapt classical nucleation theory and Cahn-Hilliard-type equations for spinodal decomposition, using local thermodynamical variables (density, pressure, velocity dispersion) from the hydro simulation to compute fragmentation timescales and mass spectra. This will be calibrated and tested in high-resolution 'zoom-in' simulations from the MillenniumTNG project, comparing the resulting star formation and black hole accretion histories to standard prescriptions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, thermodynamically consistent model for gas fragmentation in galaxy formation, moving beyond phenomenological density thresholds. It will directly link the statistical properties of the star-forming interstellar medium to its large-scale cosmological environment and offer new insights into the origin of the stellar initial mass function and the early growth of black holes.
proposals.novelty: While phase transitions are mentioned in astrophysics, applying the formal mathematical machinery of condensed matter nucleation and phase separation dynamics to cosmological galaxy formation simulations is a novel synthesis. It leverages Dr. Springel's expertise in simulation methodology to bridge two traditionally separate physics disciplines.

proposals.title: Analog Cosmological Structure Formation: A Laboratory Platform for Validating Gravity and Dark Matter Models
proposals.motivation: Cosmological simulations like MillenniumTNG are essential for interpreting large-scale structure, but they rely on approximations for complex, non-linear physics like dynamical friction and feedback. A controlled, physical analog system could provide a crucial testbed for these numerical models, offering direct validation of gravitational collapse and clustering principles in a regime where initial conditions and interactions are precisely known.
proposals.hypothesis: That a laboratory-scale system of gravitationally interacting particles (e.g., magnetically levitated diamagnetic spheres or cold atoms in tailored potentials) can exhibit statistical clustering and phase-space evolution analogous to cosmological dark matter halos, providing a direct experimental benchmark for N-body algorithms.
proposals.method: We will design an experimental platform using a large ensemble of laser-cooled particles in an ultra-high vacuum optical dipole trap, simulating a 2D 'sheet' universe. Their mutual interactions will be mediated by a precisely tunable, Yukawa-like potential created via dynamically screened optical forces. The system's evolution will be tracked with high-speed imaging and compared in detail to Arepo simulations, where we will implement identical initial conditions and force laws, using the same tree-PM and time-integration algorithms from our previous black hole dynamical friction work.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel experimental pipeline for validating cosmological simulation codes at a fundamental level. It will produce the first direct, quantitative tests of N-body integration accuracy and force softening in a controlled, repeatable setting, informing future improvements to large-scale simulations of galaxy formation and dark matter.
proposals.novelty: While analog gravity systems exist, none have been explicitly designed as a one-to-one experimental counterpart to cosmological N-body simulations for code validation. The novelty lies in the direct coupling of a tunable laboratory experiment to the identical numerical framework used in state-of-the-art cosmic simulations.

proposals.title: The Cosmic Dissolution Engine: Quantifying the Gravitational Smoothing of Structure in High-Resolution Cosmological Simulations
proposals.motivation: Cosmological simulations have overwhelmingly focused on the hierarchical growth of structure from initial density perturbations. However, the inverse process—the gravitational dissolution, disruption, and smoothing of structures through mergers, tidal stripping, and dynamical relaxation—is equally fundamental to shaping the cosmic web and establishing the present-day galaxy population. A systematic, quantitative study of this 'erosion' phase is critical for interpreting observations of stellar halos, intracluster light, and the demographics of satellite galaxies, yet it remains poorly characterized.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dissolution of cosmic structure is not a simple, passive erosion but a governed process where the rate of mass loss and phase-space mixing can be predicted from the specific energy and angular momentum of the accreted material, modulated by the evolving gravitational potential of the host halo.
proposals.method: We will leverage the new, high-resolution MillenniumTNG simulation suite, extending its analysis framework to track dissolution. Using the AREPO code's Lagrangian tracer particles, we will tag mass belonging to identified subhalos at accretion and follow its subsequent dispersal. We will develop and apply a new 'phase-space deconvolution' algorithm within the SUBFIND-HBT+ hierarchy to statistically separate and quantify the smooth halo component originating from dissolved structures versus the primordial background. The analysis will be calibrated against controlled N-body experiments of idealized mergers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, quantitative model for the mass loss and phase-space mixing rates of accreted structures in a full cosmological context. It will deliver a calibrated prescription for dissolution timescales as a function of host halo mass, redshift, and satellite properties, directly usable in semi-analytic models of galaxy formation. This will significantly improve predictions for observable signatures like stellar halos and the satellite luminosity function.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies examined tidal stripping in individual mergers, this proposal systematically inverts the standard structure formation paradigm to model dissolution as a primary, governed process across cosmic time, using novel phase-space analysis tools on the latest generation of flagship simulations.

proposals.title: The Role of Magnetic Fields in Driving AGN Fueling: A Cosmological MHD Simulation Study with AREPO
proposals.motivation: Understanding the mechanisms that drive gas from galactic scales onto supermassive black holes (SMBHs) is a central problem in galaxy formation. While gravitational torques and dynamical friction are well-studied, the contribution of non-gravitational physics, particularly magnetic fields, remains poorly quantified in a full cosmological context. Resolving this is critical for accurately modeling AGN feedback, which is essential for reproducing observed galaxy properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that magnetic fields, through processes like the magneto-rotational instability (MRI) and magnetic braking, significantly enhance the angular momentum transport and inflow rate of cold gas towards the galactic center, acting in concert with or even dominating purely gravitational mechanisms in certain regimes.
proposals.method: We will employ the cosmological magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) code AREPO, extending the successful 'Arkenstone' simulation framework. We will run a suite of high-resolution zoom-in simulations of massive galaxy formation, varying the initial magnetic field strength and geometry. Key diagnostics will include tracking gas inflow rates, magnetic stress tensors, and the dynamics of cold gas streams and the circumnuclear disk, comparing runs with and without MHD physics to isolate magnetic effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, cosmological quantification of magnetic field contributions to SMBH fueling rates. It will deliver a calibrated sub-grid model for AGN accretion that incorporates magnetic torques, directly improving the physical basis for feedback models in large-volume simulations like MillenniumTNG.
proposals.novelty: While idealized MHD studies of accretion disks exist, this proposal uniquely integrates MHD into a state-of-the-art cosmological galaxy formation simulation to assess the role of magnetic fields from the circumgalactic medium down to the unresolved accretion flow, a scale not previously connected in this context.

proposals.title: A Combustion-Inspired Subgrid Model for Turbulent Multiphase Quasar Winds in Cosmological Simulations
proposals.motivation: Cosmological simulations like MillenniumTNG and Arkenstone struggle to resolve the multiphase, turbulent structure of quasar-driven winds, which are crucial for galaxy evolution. Current subgrid models for stellar and AGN feedback often treat the wind as a single-phase fluid, missing the complex mixing and cooling between hot wind fluid and entrained cold clouds that regulates momentum transfer and mass loading.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying well-tested turbulence and mixing models from combustion engineering—specifically, models for flame propagation in turbulent media—can provide a physically grounded subgrid description for the multiphase structure, cooling, and momentum evolution of quasar winds in cosmological volumes.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Turbulent Flame Speed Closure (TFC) model and the Linear Eddy Model (LEM) for large-eddy simulation from combustion theory. These will be implemented as new modules within the AREPO code, treating the wind as a turbulent mixture of hot and cold phases. The model will track a progress variable (akin to a 'fuel fraction') for cold gas mass fraction, with subgrid mixing rates and effective cooling set by local turbulent properties derived from the resolved flow. Calibration will use high-resolution 'wind tunnel' simulations of cloud-crushing problems.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first physically motivated, turbulence-aware subgrid model for multiphase quasar winds in large-scale cosmological simulations. It will improve predictions for wind mass loading, metal transport, and the impact of feedback on galaxy formation, directly addressing uncertainties highlighted in recent Arkenstone results.
proposals.novelty: While turbulence models exist in astrophysics, the direct translation of combustion engineering frameworks—which explicitly model the interaction of phases in a turbulent, energy-releasing flow—to cosmological quasar winds is a novel cross-disciplinary approach not yet explored in the field.

proposals.title: An Agent-Based Model for Supermassive Black Hole Ecology: Coupling Growth, Feedback, and Galactic Environments
proposals.motivation: Current cosmological simulations model supermassive black hole (SMBH) growth and feedback via subgrid prescriptions tied to local gas properties, which can oversimplify the complex, non-local interplay between SMBHs and their galactic hosts. This limits our understanding of how stochastic accretion, dynamical friction, and feedback energy deposition collectively regulate galaxy evolution. A new modeling paradigm is needed to capture these emergent, multi-scale interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling SMBHs as autonomous agents, whose growth and feedback rules are governed by their dynamical state and a resolved galactic environment, will produce more realistic and diverse evolutionary pathways for SMBH-galaxy co-evolution than current continuous subgrid models.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) framework, integrated into the AREPO code, where each SMBH is an agent with internal states (mass, spin, orbit) and behavioral rules for accretion (stochastic Bondi-Hoyle with duty cycles) and feedback (energy/momentum injection based on specific energy, inspired by Arkenstone). The galactic environment (gas density, velocity, potential) will be provided by the live hydrodynamical simulation. Agent interactions include dynamical friction (using the calibrated N-body model) and mergers. We will implement and test the ABM within high-resolution zoom-in simulations of Milky Way-mass halos.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, flexible framework for SMBH modeling that naturally incorporates stochasticity and history-dependent behavior. It will yield new predictions for SMBH occupation fractions, scaling relations, and the variability of feedback impacts across different galactic environments, directly testable with upcoming surveys.
proposals.novelty: The application of agent-based modeling, a technique from ecology and social sciences, to the physics of SMBHs in a live cosmological simulation is a fundamentally new approach. It moves beyond prescribed, continuous subgrid models by treating black holes as discrete decision-making entities within their ecosystem.

proposals.title: The Impact of Extreme Feedback Prescriptions on Large-Scale Structure: A Systematic Study with MillenniumTNG
proposals.motivation: Subgrid models for stellar and AGN feedback are critical yet uncertain components of cosmological hydrodynamical simulations, directly influencing galaxy formation and the distribution of baryons. While feedback is known to regulate star formation, its precise impact on the large-scale clustering of matter, galaxies, and the intracluster medium remains poorly quantified, creating a major systematic for interpreting upcoming Stage-IV cosmological surveys like Euclid and LSST.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the predicted two-point correlation functions of galaxies, dark matter, and the thermal Sunyaev-Zel'dovich (tSZ) effect are highly sensitive not just to the strength, but to the fundamental *mode* of feedback energy deposition (e.g., high-specific-energy winds vs. thermal dumping), with distinct imprints on scales from 1 Mpc/h to 100 Mpc/h.
proposals.method: We will employ the high-resolution, large-volume MillenniumTNG simulation framework. Using the AREPO code and the 'Arkenstone' galaxy formation model as a baseline, we will implement a suite of controlled variations. These will include: 1) a 'preventive' model emphasizing high-specific-energy, low-mass-loading galactic winds; 2) a 'curative' model with strong, delayed thermal AGN feedback; and 3) a radically 'weak' feedback model. We will quantify differences using power spectra, correlation functions, and halo occupation distributions, leveraging the project's existing ray-tracing and Born-approximation pipelines for consistent weak lensing and tSZ map generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative error budget for how feedback uncertainty propagates to cosmological observables. It will identify which clustering statistics are most robust to feedback prescriptions and which are most degenerate, directly informing the analysis of next-generation sky surveys. The resulting public simulation suite will serve as a benchmark for testing galaxy formation models against large-scale structure.
proposals.novelty: While feedback has been varied within 'reasonable' ranges, this study systematically pushes implementations to their physical extremes within a state-of-the-art, large-volume cosmological framework, explicitly connecting subgrid physics to precision cosmology metrics rather than just galaxy properties.

proposals.title: The Gpc^3 MillenniumTNG Run: Cosmological Constraints from the Largest-Scale Clustering and Baryon Acoustic Oscillations
proposals.motivation: Current flagship cosmological hydrodynamical simulations like IllustrisTNG and MillenniumTNG are limited to volumes of ~(300 Mpc)^3, which are insufficient to capture the largest-scale clustering modes and suffer from significant sample variance. This impedes precise theoretical predictions for next-generation wide-field surveys like Euclid and the Vera C. Rubin Observatory LSST, which will map the cosmic web over Gpc scales.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Gpc-scale simulation, even at reduced mass resolution, will provide a more accurate and less variance-limited prediction for the clustering power spectrum, particularly for the baryon acoustic oscillation (BAO) feature and non-Gaussian signatures on the largest scales, enabling a robust confrontation with upcoming observational data.
proposals.method: We will extend the successful MillenniumTNG framework to a (2.1 Gpc)^3 volume using the AREPO moving-mesh code, with a gravity-only dark matter particle mass of ~10^10 M_sun. The initial conditions will be generated with the same cosmological parameters as MTNG, and the simulation will be run on a next-generation HPC system like SuperMUC-NG. We will employ the L-Gadget3 tree-PM code for gravity and post-process the outputs with the SUBFIND algorithm to identify halos, enabling a direct comparison of clustering statistics with the smaller, higher-resolution MTNG runs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first Gpc-scale simulation within the established TNG model family, creating a definitive theoretical reference for the clustering of matter and halos on the largest observable scales. It will quantify the reduction in sample variance for power spectrum and bispectrum measurements, directly informing the analysis strategies of Euclid and LSST for extracting cosmological parameters.
proposals.novelty: While large-volume, low-resolution N-body runs exist, this proposal uniquely applies the well-calibrated, multi-physics TNG galaxy formation model—including AGN and stellar feedback—to a Gpc volume, allowing a self-consistent study of how baryonic processes modulate the largest-scale clustering in a way that pure dark matter simulations cannot.

proposals.title: Constraining Primordial Fluctuations with AREPO: An Inverse Approach to Cosmological Initial Conditions from z=0 Galaxy Surveys
proposals.motivation: Current cosmological analyses rely on forward-modeling initial conditions and comparing to observations via summary statistics, which discards significant information in the non-linear cosmic web. Directly inferring the primordial density field from the observed late-time universe would provide a powerful, maximally informative constraint on cosmology and test of the gravitational instability paradigm. This is a formidable inverse problem requiring a high-fidelity, differentiable model of non-linear gravitational and baryonic evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: The AREPO code's accurate hydrodynamics and gravitational solver, combined with adjoint differentiation techniques, can be used to construct a differentiable forward model capable of inverting the observed low-redshift galaxy distribution to recover the underlying linear initial conditions and cosmological parameters.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel framework by integrating the AREPO simulation code with a machine learning-based adjoint state method. The forward model will use the AREPO solver within the cosmological simulation framework of the MillenniumTNG project. We will implement automatic differentiation through the key physical steps (gravity, hydrodynamics, sub-grid models for star formation and feedback) using a custom-built computational graph. A gradient-based optimization (e.g., L-BFGS) will iteratively adjust the initial Gaussian random field to minimize the discrepancy between the simulated final density field and observational data from surveys like DESI or Euclid, leveraging the gradients computed via the adjoint method.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-precision map of inferred linear density fluctuations from a hydrodynamical reconstruction, offering a new direct probe of primordial non-Gaussianity. It will establish a novel analysis pipeline that maximizes the cosmological information extracted from galaxy surveys, moving beyond two-point statistics to a full-field Bayesian inference framework.
proposals.novelty: While Lagrangian perturbation theory reconstructions exist, this proposal is novel in using a full, differentiable hydrodynamical solver (AREPO) for the inverse problem, explicitly incorporating the effects of baryonic physics and non-linear structure formation beyond the Born approximation in a self-consistent manner.

proposals.title: A Stationary-Mesh AREPO Framework for High-Resolution Studies of Steady-State Stellar Winds and Their Galactic Impact
proposals.motivation: Stellar winds from massive stars and AGB stars are a critical source of momentum, energy, and metal-enriched gas for the interstellar medium, directly influencing galaxy formation and evolution. However, cosmological simulations like MillenniumTNG and feedback models like Arkenstone typically treat these winds via sub-grid approximations due to the vast scale separation between stellar surfaces and galactic scales. A method to self-consistently simulate these steady-state outflows at high resolution within a galactic context is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: By operating the moving-mesh code AREPO in a novel stationary-mesh mode, we can achieve the high numerical accuracy and resolution needed to capture the detailed physics of steady stellar winds, and that coupling these resolved wind solutions to a larger-scale galactic simulation will significantly alter the thermodynamics and chemistry of the circumgalactic medium compared to standard sub-grid feedback prescriptions.
proposals.method: We will develop a stationary-mesh operational mode for AREPO, fixing the mesh geometry to a high-resolution region around a star while allowing flow across cell interfaces, leveraging its existing finite-volume Godunov solver and gradient reconstruction. This setup will be initialized with stellar boundary conditions to model thermally and radiation-driven winds to a steady state. The resulting wind properties (mass loss, energy, velocity) will then be used to formulate an improved, physically-grounded sub-grid model, which will be implemented and tested in full cosmological 'zoom-in' simulations of galaxy formation, directly comparing to the ad-hoc winds in the Arkenstone model.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first high-fidelity, mesh-based simulations of steady stellar winds directly connectable to cosmological galaxy formation simulations. It will produce a new, calibrated feedback prescription for stellar winds based on first-principles hydrodynamics, reducing a major uncertainty in models of galactic outflows and the baryon cycle.
proposals.novelty: While AREPO's moving mesh is famed for cosmological structure formation, using it in a deliberately stationary mode to study steady astrophysical flows is a novel inversion of its standard paradigm. This approach combines the code's inherent advantages for fluid dynamics with the controlled environment needed for wind physics, bridging a critical gap between stellar and galactic scales.

proposals.title: Cosmological Hydrodynamics for Laboratory Plasmas: A GADGET-3 Application to Supersonic Flows in High-Energy-Density Experiments
proposals.motivation: High-energy-density (HED) laboratory experiments, such as those on laser facilities like NIF or OMEGA, produce complex, turbulent, supersonic plasma flows relevant to astrophysical phenomena. However, simulating these experiments requires methods that handle multi-scale, multi-material hydrodynamics with strong shocks and radiative cooling, challenges directly analogous to those in cosmological galaxy formation. Pre-2026, the specialized codes for HED physics and cosmological hydro have evolved largely in isolation, missing a key cross-disciplinary opportunity.
proposals.hypothesis: The moving-mesh, Lagrangian hydrodynamics scheme of the cosmological code GADGET-3, particularly its SPH implementation with sophisticated sub-grid models for cooling and feedback, can be adapted and validated to accurately model the supersonic, radiative flows generated in laser-driven laboratory plasma experiments.
proposals.method: We will adapt the GADGET-3 codebase, replacing its cosmological gravity and expansion terms with a laboratory-frame setup. The core SPH (Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics) solver and its ANARCHY variant, well-tested for multiphase galactic winds, will be used as the hydrodynamic engine. We will implement equation-of-state and optically thin radiative cooling modules appropriate for high-Z laboratory plasmas. The simulation will be initialized using conditions from published OMEGA experiments studying radiative shock interactions and jet collimation, with validation against experimental interferometry and X-ray data.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel application of a mature cosmological tool to laboratory astrophysics, providing a new, Lagrangian simulation capability for HED plasmas. It will test the universality of astrophysical hydro methods and potentially offer improved handling of mixing and interface instabilities in laboratory experiments. A successful adaptation would create a powerful bridge between computational cosmology and experimental plasma physics.
proposals.novelty: While cosmological codes simulate similar physics (e.g., shocks, cooling), applying them directly to controlled laboratory experiments with precise initial conditions for code validation is a novel inversion of the typical 'astrophysics-inspired' laboratory study. The explicit use of GADGET-3's well-developed feedback and wind algorithms to model laser-energy deposition is a non-obvious methodological crossover.

ideas.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Framework to Identify and Quantify Non-Ideal EOS Effects in Intracluster Plasma Observables
ideas.description: This research develops a modular equation of state (EOS) framework for the intracluster medium that incorporates parameterized non-ideal effects including Coulomb interactions, partial degeneracy, and relativistic corrections. A core innovation is a Bayesian model selection pipeline that uses synthetic observables generated from high-resolution cosmological simulations to quantify which physical deviations from an ideal gas are both significant and detectable with existing and imminent instrumentation. The approach produces falsifiable predictions and clear observational signatures, moving beyond a simple replacement to provide a principled method for EOS selection and validation against multi-wavelength data.

ideas.title: Adapting Chemical Reaction Rate Theory to Model Entropy Generation in Astrophysical Flows
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard assumption of applying entropy conservation everywhere except at discontinuous jumps. By analogy to chemical kinetics, where detailed reaction rate theory explicitly models entropy production in irreversible processes, we propose a shift in astrophysical fluid modeling. The research would focus on developing microphysical rate equations for key dissipative processes within shocks and cooling flows—treating these regions not as infinitesimal boundaries but as finite volumes of non-equilibrium activity. The solution from reaction engineering is adapted by creating sub-grid models that calculate entropy generation rates from first principles (e.g., collision integrals, emission/absorption probabilities, viscous dissipation scales), then integrating these to obtain the net entropy production. This transforms the modeling paradigm from 'conservation plus prescribed jumps' to a direct, physics-based computation of entropy generation in dissipative regions.

ideas.title: Asynchronous, Spatially-Aware Adaptive Time Integration for Multi-Physics Hydrodynamics
ideas.description: This research develops a novel time integration framework that overcomes the fatal flaw of decoupled time and space adaptation. It introduces a joint space-time error estimator that triggers both mesh refinement and local timestep selection based on the evolution of multi-physics state variables. The core innovation is an asynchronous task-based algorithm that allows different spatial regions to advance with their own optimal timesteps, managed via a global priority queue and conservative flux corrections at time-step boundaries. This approach directly addresses the core instability in simulating events like supernovae, where burning fronts, shocks, and rarefactions interact across widely varying timescales, leading to a more robust and computationally efficient method than conventional adaptive time-stepping alone.

ideas.title: Apply corrected kernel mesh-free methods from cardiac electrophysiology to cosmological instability modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts mesh-free Galerkin methods with corrected kernels from cardiac electrophysiology to cosmological instability modeling. In cardiac electrophysiology, reproducing kernel particle methods and other corrected kernel approaches were developed to accurately simulate electrical wave propagation and spiral wave breakup in complex cardiac tissue geometries, overcoming numerical artifacts from traditional mesh-based methods while handling discontinuities. By transferring these kernel correction techniques to cosmology, we can develop mesh-free methods that maintain consistency and stability when modeling gravitational instabilities, cosmic shocks, and baryonic feedback processes in the formation of the cosmic web. This approach would provide improved numerical accuracy for discontinuities and sharp features in large-scale structure formation while avoiding the constraints of fixed spatial meshes, similar to how these methods successfully handled wavefront discontinuities in biological tissue simulations.

ideas.title: Adaptive Time-Stepping with Embedded Error Control Inspired by Stiff Chemical Kinetics
ideas.description: The original idea seeks to improve adaptive time-stepping for highly dynamic gas physics like supernovae. An analogous problem exists in computational chemistry, simulating stiff reaction kinetics with explosive ignition events. There, Rosenbrock-W methods solve the stiffness by using linearly implicit schemes with embedded error estimation for automatic step-size control. Adapting this solution, the research will develop a new hydrodynamical time-integrator that embeds a low-order and a high-order linearly implicit step. By comparing their results within each computational step, the scheme obtains a reliable local error estimate to dynamically adjust the time-step. This will allow simulations of supernovae to maintain accuracy during the intensely fast processes of core bounce and shock propagation while efficiently using larger steps during the slower evolutionary phases, improving both robustness and computational efficiency.

ideas.title: A Conserved, Multi-Resolution SPH Framework Using Overlapping Particle Sets and Resolution-Aware Kernels
ideas.description: This system overcomes the conservation and noise issues of particle splitting/merging by employing multiple, concurrently active SPH particle sets that exist in the same domain at different resolutions, defined by an underlying hierarchical spatial index. Each resolution level is represented by its own set of particles, which overlap in regions requiring high fidelity. The fluid equations are solved in a unified manner across all sets using resolution-adapted smoothing kernels and rigorously derived coupling forces at resolution boundaries. This ensures exact mass, momentum, and energy conservation while providing the local resolution enhancement of AMR, without the disruptive creation or destruction of Lagrangian particles.

ideas.title: Adversarially-Stressed Validation of a Moving Mesh Method: From Benchmarks to Predictive Multi-Physics Simulation
ideas.description: This research rigorously validates a new moving mesh method through a tiered adversarial framework. First, standard hydrodynamical test problems establish baseline correctness. Second, a bespoke suite of 'adversarial' numerical stress tests, targeting mesh tangling, conservation errors under extreme deformation, and coupling with source terms, probes specific weaknesses. Finally, the method's predictive capability is tested by simulating a complex, diagnosable laboratory astrophysics experiment (e.g., a shock-driven interface instability), with quantitative comparison to experimental data. This approach ensures robustness not just on benchmarks, but for challenging real-world applications.

ideas.title: Adaptive Hybrid Mesh Strategy Using Selective Motion Triggered by Feature Sensors
ideas.description: This research investigates adaptive hybrid stationary and moving mesh strategies for computational fluid dynamics, inspired by adaptive optics in astronomy. The analogous solution uses a sensor to detect localized atmospheric distortion and selectively corrects only those regions with a deformable mirror. Adapted to this context, the idea is to develop a scheme where mesh motion is enabled only in specific, dynamically identified regions like shocks or intense shear layers. A real-time feature detection sensor will monitor the solution field and trigger localized mesh deformation or motion when a gradient-based threshold is exceeded, confining computational expense and potential mesh distortion to areas of high activity while the bulk of the domain remains on a stable, stationary grid. This combines the accuracy of moving meshes for capturing sharp features with the efficiency and robustness of stationary meshes for smooth flow regions.

ideas.title: Apply adaptive particle splitting/merging inspired by multi-scale biological modeling to SPH
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational systems biology, where multi-scale simulations dynamically manage resolution by splitting or merging agent-based entities (like cells) based on local activity. The analogous problem of managing computational cost while resolving fine details in a particle/agent system is solved there through coarse-graining and refinement rules. Applying this to SPH involves developing rigorous criteria and kernels for dynamically splitting SPH particles in high-gradient regions (e.g., shocks, interfaces) and merging them in smooth regions. This creates an adaptive resolution mechanism within the meshless SPH method, mirroring the efficiency gains of AMR in grid-based codes but operating on the Lagrangian particle ensemble itself.

ideas.title: Physics-Blended Hybrid Mesh Motion: Coupling Flow Velocity with Geometry-Driven Adaptation
ideas.description: This approach generates mesh motion using a hybrid velocity field that strategically combines the physical fluid velocity with geometry-based drivers like curvature or error indicators. A feature-sensitive controller analyzes the local flow solution to determine the optimal blend, ensuring the mesh adheres closely to material interfaces for accuracy while adapting elsewhere for quality. This preserves the core benefits of ALE methods for tracking moving boundaries and reduces numerical diffusion, while simultaneously preventing mesh degradation through targeted, geometry-aware motion. The result is a robust, adaptive mesh that remains physically consistent with the underlying flow dynamics.

ideas.title: Adaptive Approximate Riemann Solver via Machine Learning Surrogate
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that an exact Riemann solver must be used uniformly throughout a computational fluid dynamics simulation. Drawing an analogy from computational chemistry's multi-fidelity approaches to electron correlation, we propose an adaptive, surrogate-guided method. A fast approximate Riemann solver (like HLLC) will be used as the primary workhorse. A machine learning model, trained offline to predict the local error of this approximate solver relative to an exact reference, will act as a surrogate error estimator during runtime. The simulation will dynamically invoke the exact, expensive Riemann solver only at the cell interfaces where the surrogate predicts high error, thereby achieving significant computational speedup for problems where exact solutions are sparsely needed, without sacrificing overall solution accuracy.

ideas.title: Adaptive Hybrid Riemann Solver with Error-Controlled Switching for Robust Compressible Flow Simulation
ideas.description: This research develops a robust hybrid Riemann solver framework that dynamically selects the most appropriate solver type for each cell interface based on local flow conditions. An online error estimator, derived from thermodynamic or wave-strength criteria, triggers an automatic switch from a fast approximate solver to an exact or high-order solver in regions prone to approximation errors, such as near strong discontinuities. This approach guarantees that the solution maintains physical fidelity and nonlinear stability in critical regions while maximizing computational speed in smooth flow areas. The result is a method that systematically overcomes the primary failure mode of purely approximate solvers without reverting to the global use of expensive exact solvers.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Mesh Optimization via Adjoint-Guided Quality Control
ideas.description: This research develops a mesh optimization system that prevents degenerate cells while strictly preserving simulation physics. It formulates mesh smoothing as a constrained optimization problem, minimizing a composite objective of geometric irregularity and a physics distortion penalty. An adjoint sensitivity method guides the regularization, allowing aggressive quality improvements in benign regions while limiting modification near critical solution features like discontinuities. The result is a robust, automated pre-processor that enhances mesh quality for numerical stability without compromising the accuracy of the underlying computational model.

ideas.title: Optimization-Driven Global Control for Seamless Hybrid Mesh Motion
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework where mesh motion is not a binary, region-locked decision but a continuously varying field governed by an optimization principle. A control system, using adjoint-based error estimates or feature density functions, computes an optimal mesh velocity that smoothly blends high motion in critical regions with near-zero motion elsewhere, maintaining global mesh topology. The approach enforces strict discrete geometric conservation laws and solution monotonicity constraints within the optimization loop, ensuring robustness and stability. This results in a seamless adaptive strategy that dynamically prioritizes mesh resolution where needed without introducing interface errors or solver instability.

ideas.title: Communication-Aware Distributed Dynamic Voronoi Tessellation with Bounded Consistency
ideas.description: This research develops algorithms and a system architecture for dynamic Voronoi/Delaunay tessellation optimized for distributed-memory supercomputing environments. The core innovation is a domain-decomposition-based approach that minimizes synchronization by enforcing a strict spatial ownership model and employing predictive ghost region updates to contain the propagation of mesh changes. The system trades strict global consistency for bounded, asynchronous reconciliation, enabling high scalability. This results in a robust framework where computational efficiency is achieved not just through local algorithm optimization, but through a fundamental redesign that addresses the primary bottleneck of inter-node communication in a dynamic geometric context.

ideas.title: Adapting Geologic Isostatic Rebalancing for Mesh Quality Control
ideas.description: This idea adapts the geophysical solution of isostatic rebalancing—where Earth's crust gradually adjusts vertically to restore gravitational equilibrium after erosion or deposition—to the problem of preventing degenerate cells in computational meshes. The analogous geological process naturally smooths extreme topographic variations. The adapted solution creates a physics-inspired mesh regularization algorithm that treats mesh vertices as crustal blocks with simulated properties. Vertices in degenerate cells experience 'rebound' forces, while those in overly compressed regions experience 'subsidence' forces, iteratively relaxing the mesh toward a balanced state of uniform element quality, thereby preventing cell degeneration through a principle mimicking natural topographic smoothing.

ideas.title: Autonomic Runtime for Dynamic Load Balancing and Resilience on Heterogeneous Exascale Systems
ideas.description: This research designs a self-aware, hierarchical runtime system that autonomously manages parallel execution on million-core exascale architectures. It continuously monitors hardware telemetry (power, temperature, network congestion) and application progress to detect and predict performance degradation and faults. Using online machine learning models, the system dynamically re-decomposes workloads and migrates tasks at fine granularity to optimize for both performance and energy efficiency. This approach transforms static decomposition into an adaptive, resilient process that treats extreme heterogeneity and dynamic faults as intrinsic system characteristics to be managed at runtime.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Hierarchical Domain Decomposition Inspired by Urban Traffic Flow Control
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to redesign parallelization and domain decomposition for next-generation exascale supercomputers by drawing an analogy from hierarchical urban traffic flow control systems in smart cities. The analogous solution uses a multi-layer strategy: a high-level coordinator manages city-wide flow, while distributed, intelligent intersection controllers handle local, real-time adjustments based on immediate traffic conditions, with bidirectional communication between layers. Adapted to exascale computing, this inspires a novel software framework featuring a minimal global supervisor for overarching domain decomposition strategy and system-wide load monitoring, coupled with autonomous, localized load-balancing agents embedded within compute nodes or groups of nodes. These agents would have the authority to perform fine-grained task migration and resource reallocation in response to localized computational imbalances or communication bottlenecks, using local sensor data and lightweight directives from the global layer. This hybrid approach aims to solve the scalability challenge of purely centralized managers and the coordination chaos of purely decentralized systems, enabling efficient, adaptive execution on architectures with millions of heterogeneous cores by dynamically managing 'computational traffic'.
